This is 'embrace the standard vs play to strengths': OTel is the de-facto cloud-native standard, so supporting it lowers the barrier; but its generic model doesn't natively grok prompt version/token usage, where the native SDK is more precise. Langfuse does both — OTLP for reach, native SDK for the best experience.
2. A span-type observation often sends SPAN_CREATE first, then SPAN_UPDATE at the end. What's the main benefit of this 'incremental reporting'?
  1. To reduce the number of events
  2. It records 'it started' earlier, so even if it later crashes/times out you know which step stalled — failure cases don't vanish just because 'the end never came'
  3. So the SDK assembles the final state itself
  4. Because ClickHouse only accepts two writes
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Waiting to send one complete event means a mid-flight crash erases that step — yet failures are exactly what you most want to observe. Incremental reporting = 'record as it happens', and the server merges create+update into one row (L15).
3. In this 'incremental events + merge' protocol, where does the most complex merge logic live, and why?
  1. In the frontend at render time
  2. In database triggers
  3. In the SDK: each language implements merging itself
  4. On the server (worker): SDKs just emit small events; merging 'possibly out-of-order/duplicate events into one consistent row' lives in one place, avoiding each language SDK re-implementing (and re-bugging) it
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Converge complexity to one strictly testable place (the server): SDKs stay light, portable and low-impact; the error-prone merge logic is maintained once in the worker rather than scattered across every language SDK.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • If you were instrumenting your own app, would you pick the Langfuse native SDK or OpenTelemetry? How would your system's current state (already on OTel? need precise cost?) shape that choice?

The dual-store architecture

Last lesson, data left your app as "events". This lesson is about where it lands. Many assume one database suffices, but Langfuse uses four stores — Postgres, ClickHouse, Redis, S3 — each for its own job. Not showing off: different data has wildly different access patterns, and the wrong store will crush the hot path. Understand the division of labor and you understand the foundation of Langfuse's performance and scalability.

🔌 Analogy
Picture the four stores as a company's four "ways to keep things": Postgres is the filing cabinet — important records (who's an employee, project config), needing to be accurate, editable, quick to look up but not huge; ClickHouse is the giant warehouse — mountains of goods (hundreds of millions of events a day), needing bulk stock-taking by category; Redis is the conveyor belt + staging table — goods queue on the belt (queues), and frequently-used small items stay at hand (cache); S3 is off-site storage — big, rarely-flipped originals (event manuscripts, uploaded images, large exports). Each excels at its own; cram them into one and you lose.
The four stores' division of labor: Postgres=config/metadata (txn, consistent, small); ClickHouse=wide events (columnar, huge, fast aggregation); Redis=queue+cache (in-memory, fast, temporary); S3=event manuscript/media/exports (object store, big, cheap). web leans read, worker leans write, but both pick the store that fits the data.

Why not just one

The core reason in one line: transactional (OLTP) and analytical (OLAP) workloads are opposite optimization targets, and no single store nails both extremes.

  • Config/metadata (projects, users, API keys, prompt definitions) is classic OLTP: small, frequent precise single-row read/write, strong consistency (create a project, use it instantly), with foreign keys. Postgres's home turf.
  • Telemetry (trace/observation/score) is classic OLAP: enormous (hundreds of millions/day), write-mostly, queries are mostly time-window + dimensional aggregation ("cost by model over 7 days"). Columnar ClickHouse's home turf.

🗄️ Postgres is good at (OLTP)

precise single-row read/write, strong consistency, foreign keys, transactions. Fits small, important, must-be-instant config data. Bad at: aggregating scans over huge rows.

📊 ClickHouse is good at (OLAP)

time-window scans + aggregation over a few columns of huge rows, columnar + compressed. Fits massive, append-write, dimensional telemetry. Bad at: frequent single-row edits + strong consistency.

Cram telemetry into Postgres and huge rows + frequent aggregation overwhelm it; cram config into ClickHouse and its weak transactions, eventual consistency fail "instantly usable". So Langfuse isn't being greedy — it's letting each kind of data live in the store that fits it best, the storage-layer landing point of Lesson 2's "design around columnar access" and "denormalize carefully".

Two opposite access patterns: OLTP uses an index to hit exactly one row (read config, change settings); OLAP must scan a few columns of a big slice of rows and aggregate (cost by time/model). Row stores excel at the former, columnar at the latter — which is why config goes to Postgres and telemetry to ClickHouse.

What each store holds

StoreHoldsClient in the repo
Postgresorg/project/user, API keys, prompts, eval config, pricing… (control plane)packages/shared/src/db.ts (Prisma)
ClickHousetraces / observations / scores — three wide-event tables (data plane).../server/clickhouse/client.ts
RedisBullMQ queues (decouple ingestion) + cache (e.g. prompts, eval config).../server/redis/redis.ts
S3 / blobraw event manuscript (for merge/replay), multimodal media, bulk exports.../server/s3/index.ts

One detail: S3 isn't just "big-file storage". In the ingestion path, each incoming event lands in S3 first, and the worker re-reads S3 when merging (L05, L15) — here S3 plays the "event log / source of truth" role. The wide events in ClickHouse are essentially a "materialized view" of the merged S3 manuscripts: if ClickHouse data ever needs rebuilding, it can in principle be replayed from the raw S3 events. This "raw log + derived table" split is Lesson 2's "immutable / append-oriented events" in action.

control planePostgres
who can use it, configured how: org/project/user, API keys, prompts, eval config, pricing. Few but critical changes, needs strong consistency.
data planeClickHouse
the actual telemetry flood: traces/observations/scores wide-event tables. Huge, append, dimensional aggregation.
transportRedis
the queue that makes ingestion async, plus a cache for hot config. In-memory, temporary, very fast.
object storeS3 / blob
the event source of truth, uploaded media, bulk exports. Big, rarely-flipped things live here — cheap and durable.

These four layers collaborate at runtime, not in isolation. Recall Lesson 5's path: on ingestion, events first enter the Redis queue and originals land in S3; after merging the worker writes ClickHouse; and to merge it must know "which prompt version this observation links to" and "what model pricing applies", so it reads config back from Postgres. One ingestion, four stores each pulling their weight. Reads are similar: the UI list queries ClickHouse, but "what's this project called, who has access" comes from Postgres, and hot config goes through the Redis cache. So understanding Langfuse is essentially understanding how data flows among these four stores — which is why Part 2 spends a whole lesson on the storage division of labor: it's the shared foundation of every later path lesson.

🎯 Design tradeoff
Four stores = four operational burdens, so why split this much? Lesson 2 has a principle: "treat cost and operational simplicity as constraints" — each extra database means more backups, monitoring, upgrades, DR. Langfuse still keeps four because each solves a hard problem a single store can't: without ClickHouse, huge aggregation queries are unusably slow; without the Redis queue, ingestion can't go async to absorb spikes; without S3, big fields and media bloat the database and you can't replay. In other words, these four aren't "add because we can" — each earned its existence. That's also the test for "should we add another store": does the problem it solves justify its long-term operational cost?
🎯 Key points
  • Four stores, each its own job: Postgres (config/metadata·OLTP), ClickHouse (wide events·OLAP), Redis (queue+cache), S3 (event manuscript+media+exports).
  • Core reason: OLTP and OLAP are opposite targets — config needs precise single-row + strong consistency, telemetry needs massive scan + aggregation, so split stores.
  • S3 is the "source of truth": events land in S3 first; ClickHouse wide events are the merged derived table, replayable.
  • Control plane (Postgres) vs data plane (ClickHouse) is the macro version of Lesson 3's "domain shape vs physical storage".
  • Four stores aren't tech-stacking — each earned its existence, which is also the test for "should we add another store".

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why does Langfuse put config/metadata in Postgres and trace/observation/score in ClickHouse, rather than one database for all?
  1. Because ClickHouse can't store strings
  2. Purely to use more tech
  3. Because Postgres has no indexes
  4. Because they're opposite workloads: config is OLTP (small, precise single-row, strongly consistent), telemetry is OLAP (massive, time-window + dimensional aggregation); no single store nails both extremes
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Config needs precise single-row read/write and strong consistency (instantly usable) — row-store Postgres's turf; telemetry is massive, write-mostly, dimensionally aggregated — columnar ClickHouse's turf. Forcing one store loses both ways. This is 'choose storage by access pattern'.
2. What key role does S3 play in the ingestion path?
  1. The events' 'source of truth / raw log': events land in S3 first, the worker re-reads S3 to merge; ClickHouse wide events are the merged derived table, in principle replayable from S3
  2. Just stores uploaded images, unrelated to ingestion
  3. Stores user passwords
  4. Replaces Redis as the queue
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. S3 isn't just a big-file store. Events land in S3 as an immutable raw log, from which the worker merges ClickHouse wide events (derived/materialized). This is L02's 'immutable/append-oriented events', and makes data replayable.
3. Langfuse uses four stores, yet L02 says 'treat operational simplicity as a constraint'. How to reconcile? What's the test for 'should we add another store'?
  1. The frontend framework decides
  2. You may only ever use one database
  3. Each store must earn its existence — does the hard problem it solves justify its long-term ops cost (backup/monitoring/upgrade/DR)
  4. More stores are always better
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. The four aren't 'add because we can': ClickHouse solves huge aggregation, Redis solves async spike absorption, S3 solves big fields + replay — each solving what one store can't, thus earning its place. That's exactly the test for adding another store.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Imagine designing a new logging/monitoring system on a tight budget. Would you start with one Postgres, or bring in ClickHouse from day one? At what scale/query pattern does a columnar store truly 'earn its existence'?

ClickHouse & the wide-event tables

Last lesson said telemetry goes to ClickHouse. This lesson opens the hood on how the three wide-event tables (traces / observations / scores) are actually built and why. It's the must-understand storage lesson — once you grasp the table engine, ordering key and partitioning, later questions ("why are lists fast", "why is by-project + by-time so efficient", "why can data be queried while being written") all fall into place. We use the observations table as the example (CREATE TABLE in packages/shared/clickhouse/migrations/unclustered/0002_observations.up.sql).

🔌 Analogy
Picture a ClickHouse table as a giant library. Partitioning is like big shelves by publish month — for "books from June 2024" you go straight to that shelf and never touch the others; the ordering key is the arrangement within a shelf (by subject, then date), so "a subject over a time range" is just a short contiguous scan; the ReplacingMergeTree engine is a shelving rule: a new edition of the same call number replaces the old (decided by version number). Together, even a vast collection is located and counted in milliseconds.
Five kinds of columns in one wide-event row: identity/linkage (tree, lookup), inlined rich wide columns (model, cost, prompt…), semi-structured Map columns (metadata, usage/cost details), ZSTD-compressed big fields (input/output), and version columns (event_ts, is_deleted). One row holds all of Lesson 3's rich attributes.

ReplacingMergeTree: later write of the same id wins

All three tables use the ReplacingMergeTree engine. The declaration looks like:

clickhouse/migrations/unclustered/0002_observations.up.sqlENGINE
ENGINE = ReplacingMergeTree(event_ts, is_deleted)
PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(start_time)
ORDER BY (project_id, type, toDate(start_time), id)

The key is ReplacingMergeTree(event_ts, is_deleted). Its semantics: multiple rows with the identical ordering key are "replaced" down to the latest one — whoever has the larger event_ts (version timestamp) wins; is_deleted=1 marks a soft delete. This perfectly fits Lessons 5 and 6's merge model: an observation that's created then updated produces two writes (same id, different event_ts), and ClickHouse automatically lets the update overwrite the create during background merge. You needn't read the old row, edit, and write back — just append; overwrite is the engine's job. This is the storage basis that makes Lesson 2's "immutable / append-oriented events" viable.

Later write wins: for two writes with the same ordering key, the engine keeps the newer event_ts; is_deleted implements soft delete. So "edit an observation" = append a record with a larger event_ts, no read-modify-write.

Ordering key + partition: why by project_id and time

Now the other two lines: PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(start_time) and ORDER BY (project_id, type, toDate(start_time), id). They decide how data is chunked and arranged on disk — and therefore whether a query can scan less.

📅 PARTITION BY month

toYYYYMM(start_time) cuts data into one chunk per month. For "last 7 days", the engine simply skips whole irrelevant months (partition pruning) — never even reads them.

🔑 ORDER BY project_id first

The first ORDER BY column is project_id, so one project's data is physically adjacent on disk. Multi-tenant queries naturally see only their project — a contiguous range, no full-table filtering.

Scanning less is the point: partitions prune irrelevant data by month, the ordering key (project_id first) clusters the target project into a contiguous range, and columnar storage reads only the columns you want. Together, "by project, by time" — the hottest query — touches only a small slice even across hundreds of millions of rows.

Columnar + compression + indexes: making "wide" fast too

Wide rows are wide — how stay fast? Three moves, all in the CREATE TABLE:

MoveIn the schemaSolves
Columnar storagethe MergeTree family is columnarqueries read only the columns used, not paying for irrelevant big fields
ZSTD compressioninput/output … CODEC(ZSTD(3))shrinks big, repetitive prompt/output text — saves space and IO
Map columnsmetadata/usage_details … Map(...)semi-structured fields need no predefined columns — flexible yet queryable
Bloom-filter indexINDEX idx_id id TYPE bloom_filter()for exact id/trace_id lookups, quickly skip blocks not containing the target

The three tables' ordering keys differ slightly but all start with project_id, expressing the consistent "multi-tenant isolation = ordering-key prefix" idea:

TableORDER BY
traces(project_id, toDate(timestamp), id)
observations(project_id, type, toDate(start_time), id)
scores(project_id, ...)
🎯 Design tradeoff
ReplacingMergeTree's overwrite is "eventual", not "immediate" — background merge takes time, so in principle you could read the old row in the instant both writes exist but aren't merged yet. That's the price of "just append, write fast". The fix: on the query side use the FINAL keyword to force-merge before reading, or use aggregation (take the max-event_ts row per id) to guarantee the final state — moving the "dedup" cost from every write to query time (and controllable). Lesson 2 says "avoid read-time dedup's hidden cost"; Langfuse's approach is to route regular queries through aggregation/final-state views, making the cost explicit and optimizable rather than paying read-modify-write on every write. Append for throughput, move dedup to the read side and optimize it — a classic write-heavy-telemetry trade.
🎯 Key points
  • Engine = ReplacingMergeTree(event_ts, is_deleted): same ordering key, later write (larger event_ts) overwrites earlier, is_deleted soft-deletes — perfectly absorbing the create+update merge model.
  • Partition = by month (toYYYYMM), so time-window queries prune whole chunks; ordering key = project_id first, so multi-tenant queries scan one contiguous range.
  • "Scan less" via partition pruning + ordering-key locating + columnar column-selection is the key to scale efficiency.
  • Columnar + ZSTD + Map columns + bloom_filter indexes keep wide, big rows fast to query and cheap to store.
  • Tradeoff: overwrite is "eventual"; dedup cost moves from writes to queries (FINAL/aggregation), buying fast writes — avoiding read-time dedup's hidden cost.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What's the core semantic of ReplacingMergeTree(event_ts, is_deleted)? How does it absorb 'an observation created then updated'?
  1. Rows with the same ordering key collapse to the largest event_ts (is_deleted soft-deletes); create and update are two writes of the same id, and background merge auto-overwrites create with update — just append, the engine overwrites
  2. It rejects writes with duplicate ids
  3. It requires deleting the old row before writing the new one
  4. It keeps both writes forever and returns both
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. ReplacingMergeTree turns 'edit one' into 'append one with a larger event_ts', no read-modify-write. It absorbs the create+update merge model and is the storage basis for L02's 'immutable/append-oriented events'.
2. Why does observations' ORDER BY start with project_id and PARTITION BY by month? What does that do for a 'project, last 7 days' query?
  1. To make writes slower
  2. Because ClickHouse has no indexes
  3. Monthly partitions prune irrelevant months; project_id-first clusters the target project into a contiguous range; so only a small slice is scanned and columnar reads only needed columns — 'scan less' is the key at scale
  4. To mix all projects' data together
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Partition pruning + ordering-key locating + columnar column-selection make the hottest 'by project, by time' query touch only a small slice across hundreds of millions of rows. This is L02's 'columnar access: time windows + ordering keys + pruning'.
3. ReplacingMergeTree's overwrite is 'eventual' (background merge takes time). What tradeoff does this bring, and how does Langfuse handle it?
  1. You may only query once a day
  2. Before merge you might read an old row; use FINAL or aggregate (max event_ts per id) for the final state — moving dedup cost from 'every write' to 'query time, controllable', buying fast writes
  3. No tradeoff at all, always strongly consistent
  4. You must take downtime to merge
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Append-for-throughput costs 'eventual consistency'. The read side uses FINAL/aggregation for the final state, moving dedup from every write to query time and optimizing it explicitly — exactly L02's 'avoid read-time dedup's hidden cost'.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • ReplacingMergeTree turns 'edit' into 'append + background overwrite'. If your own system also 'updates the same record many times', how could this 'append-only, read-the-latest' idea help you? Can you accept its cost?

The metadata schema (Postgres)

The last two lessons starred ClickHouse (massive telemetry). This one switches to the other half: Postgres. If ClickHouse stores "what happened" (trace/observation/score), Postgres stores "how the platform is configured" — who your orgs and projects are, who has access, which API keys exist, which prompts are defined, which eval rules are set, how each model is priced. This side is the control plane — small in volume yet global in importance. Its shape is all in packages/shared/prisma/schema.prisma.

🔌 Analogy
Think of the two stores as a company's two systems: Postgres is the HR/admin system — who's an employee, in which department, with what permissions, under which policies. Not much data, but it must be accurate, editable, instantly effective (a new hire's permissions must work right away). ClickHouse is the transaction ledger — mountains of records, append-only, used later for monthly/category stats. You wouldn't write policies into the ledger, nor stuff every transaction into HR files — two kinds of data, two systems, each in its place.
Two halves: Postgres stores "how the platform is configured" — org/project/user, API keys, prompts, eval/dataset config, pricing, integrations, audit; ClickHouse stores "what actually happened" — three wide-event tables. This is the macro version of Lesson 3's "domain shape vs physical storage": one governs control, one governs data.

Postgres holds the "control plane"

Open schema.prisma and you'll see dozens of models. They group by purpose (all map to snake_case tables via @@map, with cuid primary keys):

GroupMain modelsGoverns
Identity & tenancyOrganization · Project · User · *Membership · Role · ApiKeywho the org/project is, who has what permission, which key
PromptPrompt · PromptDependency · PromptProtectedLabelsversioned prompt definitions and dependencies
Datasets / evalDataset · DatasetItem · DatasetRuns · DatasetRunItems · ScoreConfig · EvalTemplate · JobConfiguration · JobExecutionthe definition & orchestration of test sets, score configs, eval jobs
Models & billingModel · Price · PricingTiermodel pricing tables (basis for cost computation, L16)
Integrations & ops*Integration · BatchExport · BatchAction · Media · AuditLog · BackgroundMigrationoutbound integrations, exports, media, audit, background migrations

See the pattern? These are all "config" and "definition", not "mountains of events". They number in the thousands to millions, are read/written precisely and often one at a time (create a project, edit a prompt, check a key's validity), and need strong consistency — exactly the relational database Postgres's forte.

✅ Config in Postgres

You want foreign keys (project → its prompts/keys/config), transactions (a multi-table change all-or-nothing), and "readable right after writing". All relational-DB strengths.

❌ Config not in ClickHouse

ClickHouse has weak transactions, eventual consistency, and dislikes frequent single-row edits. Put "create-project-instantly-usable" there and you'd get the awkward "just created but can't find it". Config there is a mismatch.

tier 1identity & tenancy
Organization / Project / User / Membership / Role / ApiKey — decide "who, in which project, with what permission". The prerequisite for everything else.
tier 2feature config
Prompt, Dataset, ScoreConfig, EvalTemplate/JobConfiguration, Model/Price — each feature's "definition & orchestration", all hung under a project.
tier 3integrations & ops
the *Integration models, BatchExport/Action, Media, AuditLog, BackgroundMigration — outbound connections and the platform's own ops records.

Project is the hub

Among these dozens of models, Project is the undeniable center. Open its definition and you'll find few fields (id, orgId, name, deletedAt soft-delete, retentionDays…) but dozens of relations — almost everything else belongs to some project:

Project is the center of the control plane: under Organization, almost everything (API keys, members, prompts, datasets/eval, models/pricing, dashboards, automation, integrations, media/export) hangs off some project 1:N. So project is the natural "data boundary" — an API key belongs to a project, and all the data you query is scoped to its project (multi-tenancy, next lesson).

"Project is the hub" has two very real consequences. One is cascade: delete a project and its keys, prompts, datasets and config are cleaned up with it (many relations are marked onDelete: Cascade), making "delete a project" a clean operation rather than leaving orphan data everywhere. The other is boundary equals permission: because everything hangs under a project, authorization only needs to answer "do you belong to this project" — the basis for the next lesson's multi-tenancy and for L21's tRPC middleware protectedProjectProcedure. Remember this "project-centric" picture and many seemingly-scattered features later turn out to hang off it.

Why are there trace tables in Postgres?

Browsing schema.prisma you may be puzzled: why are there LegacyPrismaTrace, LegacyPrismaObservation, LegacyPrismaScore? Isn't telemetry all in ClickHouse? The answer is in the name — Legacy. This is a historical remnant of Langfuse's early days storing trace/observation/score in Postgres; later, to handle scale, the analytical data moved to ClickHouse (the very reason for Lessons 7 and 8). These legacy models remain mainly for compatibility and migration — no longer the hot path. See a Legacy prefix and you know "that's the old way, don't copy it".

API keys & permissions (preview)

In the identity group, ApiKey has a security detail worth remembering now:

packages/shared/prisma/schema.prismamodel ApiKey
publicKey            // public key, plaintext
hashedSecretKey      // hash of the secret (no plaintext!)
fastHashedSecretKey  // a hash for fast verification
displaySecretKey     // a masked fragment, UI display only
scope                // ApiKeyScope, default PROJECT

Note: the secret is never stored in plaintext, only hashed — so even a DB leak yields no usable key. Permissions use the Role enum: OWNER / ADMIN / MEMBER / VIEWER / NONE, combined with org-level and project-level memberships to decide "who can do what in which project". Auth and permissions get their own lessons (L49, L53); for now just register the impression: the control plane stores not only "config" but "who may touch that config".

🎯 Design tradeoff
What's the biggest payoff of splitting "control plane" and "data plane" into two databases? Letting each evolve and scale by its own rules. The control plane is small and needs strong consistency — fine for a single (or primary/replica) Postgres; the data plane is a flood needing a horizontally-scalable ClickHouse cluster. Force them into one store and you either shortchange config consistency or crush telemetry throughput. The cost: some operations span two stores (e.g. ingestion reads model pricing and prompt links back from Postgres) — but this "read config, write data" cross-store collaboration is controllable and heavily cached, far more realistic than "making one store great at both OLTP and OLAP". Split by responsibility; let each store do only what it's best at.
🎯 Key points
  • Postgres = control plane: stores "how the platform is configured" — org/project/user, API keys, prompts, eval/dataset config, model pricing, integrations, audit. Defined in schema.prisma.
  • Project is the hub: few fields but dozens of relations; almost everything belongs 1:N to a project — the natural data boundary (multi-tenancy basis).
  • LegacyPrismaTrace/Observation/Score are historical remnants: analytical data long moved to ClickHouse; these remain only for compatibility/migration.
  • API keys are stored hashed, never plaintext; permissions use Role (OWNER/ADMIN/MEMBER/VIEWER/NONE) + org/project memberships (L49, L53).
  • Control plane vs data plane is the macro version of Lesson 3's "domain shape vs physical storage": split by responsibility, each doing what it's best at.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Why does Langfuse keep config/metadata (org/project/user, API keys, prompts, pricing…) in Postgres rather than in ClickHouse with telemetry?
  1. Because Postgres is slower, fit for rarely-used data
  2. Pure history, no reason
  3. Config needs foreign keys, transactions and strong consistency (instantly usable) — relational Postgres's strength; ClickHouse has weak transactions, eventual consistency and dislikes frequent single-row edits, so config there is a mismatch
  4. Because ClickHouse is too small
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. Config is OLTP: small, precise single-row, strongly consistent, with foreign keys — Postgres's turf; ClickHouse fits massive append + aggregation (OLAP). Split by responsibility, each at its best — a continuation of L07.
2. Why is Project the 'hub' of the control plane, and why is it the multi-tenant 'data boundary'?
  1. Because Project stores all traces
  2. Because it has no relations
  3. Because the Project table has the most rows
  4. Project has few fields but dozens of 1:N relations; almost everything (keys/members/prompts/datasets/dashboards/integrations…) belongs to a project; an API key belongs to a project and queries are scoped to it
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Almost all control-plane objects hang 1:N off a project, making it the center; and since keys and data are scoped by project, project is the natural isolation boundary (matching ClickHouse's project_id-first ordering key). Multi-tenancy next lesson.
3. Why are there still LegacyPrismaTrace/Observation/Score in schema.prisma? And how are API keys stored?
  1. Legacy back up ClickHouse; API keys aren't stored
  2. Legacy are the primary tables; API keys are stored in plaintext
  3. Both have been removed from the code
  4. Legacy models are historical remnants of storing telemetry in Postgres; analytical data moved to ClickHouse, kept only for compat/migration; API key secrets are stored hashed, never plaintext
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Legacy prefix = old way: telemetry long moved to ClickHouse (the reason for L07/L08); these remain for compat/migration. API keys store only hashedSecretKey etc., so a DB leak yields no usable key — a security detail expanded in L49/L53.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Langfuse splits 'config' and 'data' into two databases. If your system also had 'small critical config' + 'massive events', how would you divide them? How would you tame the cross-store config-read cost (hint: caching)?

Multi-tenancy

Last lesson called project the hub and the "data boundary". This lesson makes that concrete: Langfuse is a multi-tenant platform — one deployment serves thousands of unrelated teams at once, isolating their data cleanly via three nesting levels: organization → project → environment. Grasp these three and you understand "why you only see your own data" and "why the ClickHouse ordering key must start with project_id".

🔌 Analogy
Picture multi-tenancy as an office building: the organization is the company renting a floor — signs the lease, pays the bills, manages its staff (members and billing live here); the project is a department with its own area and access control, whose stuff never wanders into another department; the environment is a further "production / test area" split inside the department — the same people keep live and test data apart. Everyone may be in one building (one deployment, one database), but layered access controls keep them from crossing over.
Three nesting levels: one organization holds multiple projects, each project may split into multiple environments (e.g. production / staging / development, default default). The org manages billing and members, the project is the hard data boundary, and the environment is a soft slice within a project.

What each level governs

LevelGovernsIsolation strength
Organizationbilling, members, SSO, org-level API keysthe outer "account" boundary
Projectwhere data actually belongs: trace/observation/score, prompts, config all hang under a projecthard isolation: invisible across projects
Environmentdistinguishes production / staging / development within one projectsoft slice: queryable together or filtered by env

The key distinction is the last two: project is a "hard boundary" — hold one project's key and you simply cannot see another project's data; environment is a "soft slice" — within one project it tags live vs test data apart, so you can view only production, or compare several environments together. Why? Because "different teams' data must never mix" is a security red line (hard isolation), while "live vs test" is just one team's own view preference (soft slice) — different strengths, different treatment.

A concrete scenario for all three levels. A company (organization) signs up and pays on Langfuse, inviting 5 engineers as members. They open two projects (project): "support bot" and "doc Q&A". The two projects' data is mutually invisible — querying the support bot never mixes in doc-Q&A traces. Within "support bot" they use environment to separate live traffic (production) from load-test/debug traffic (staging), so live cost isn't polluted by load-test data — yet they can compare the two environments together when needed. The outer "company" cares only about the bill and who's a member, holding no data directly; all data lands on some project + environment. This "org manages money and people, project manages data, environment manages views" division is the entire skeleton of multi-tenancy.

project_id is the hard isolation key, threaded throughout

Multi-tenant isolation is not as simple as adding a WHERE project_id = ? filter afterward. In Langfuse, project_id is a thread running through the whole pipeline:

project_id threads the whole pipeline: auth resolves project_id from the API key (L09: a key belongs to a project); writes stamp it on every row; ClickHouse's ordering key starts with project_id (L08); queries locate the contiguous range by it. So isolation is not an afterthought filter — it's baked into the physical layout; isolation and "fast" are the same thing.

This "project_id threads the whole pipeline" design's biggest value is turning security from "the programmer remembers to filter" into "the structure guarantees it". Consider: if isolation relied on every query remembering to add WHERE project_id = ?, then one forgotten or wrong filter could let company A see company B's data — a catastrophic bug in a multi-tenant system. Langfuse instead welds project_id into the storage and auth foundations: the repository read APIs uniformly require a project_id, and tRPC's protectedProjectProcedure middleware checks first whether you belong to this project (L21). So "see another tenant's data by accident" isn't "easy to get wrong" — there simply is no such path. That's the essential difference between "security in the structure" and "security by discipline".

environment: a soft slice within a project

environment was added later. Here's how it looks in ClickHouse — all three wide-event tables gained an environment column:

clickhouse/migrations/unclustered/0008_add_environments_column.up.sqlALTER
ALTER TABLE traces       ADD COLUMN environment LowCardinality(String) DEFAULT 'default' AFTER project_id;
ALTER TABLE observations ADD COLUMN environment LowCardinality(String) DEFAULT 'default' AFTER project_id;
ALTER TABLE scores       ADD COLUMN environment LowCardinality(String) DEFAULT 'default' AFTER project_id;

Three details: (1) the type is LowCardinality(String) — env names are a small fixed set (prod/staging/dev), and low-cardinality encoding saves space and speeds queries; (2) the default 'default' — without an explicit environment, data lands in default, backward-compatible (old data naturally belongs to default); (3) the position AFTER project_id — this only places it right after project_id in the table's column layout (purely a cosmetic column order for readability); note it is NOT added to the ordering key. So unlike project_id, environment is essentially a plain filter column on the row — a soft slice: add a condition to filter by env, or ignore it to see them together.

This neatly contrasts the two isolation mechanisms: project_id is welded into the ordering key (isolate = locate, structurally safe), while environment is just an ordinary column (filtered at query time). Reasonably so — hard isolation must be guaranteed by structure, while a soft slice is just a convenient view dimension that needn't (and shouldn't) wield the heavy weapon of the ordering key.

🔒 project = hard isolation

A security red line: different projects' data is never visible to each other. Welded into the ordering-key prefix + API-key ownership, guaranteed from storage to auth.

🏷️ environment = soft slice

A view preference: within one project, tag data prod/staging/dev — separable or combinable, just one filter dimension at query time.

How one request resolves the tenant

1

carry a credential

the SDK uses an API key (writes), or a logged-in user carries a session (reads), to make a request.

2

resolve the project

the server resolves "which project" from the key/session and checks access (L09 ApiKey, L21 protectedProjectProcedure).

3

all reads/writes lock to that project

writes stamp project_id on every row; queries put project_id in the ordering-key prefix. You cannot bypass it to see another project.

4

optional: slice by environment

within that project, filter prod / staging by environment as needed.

🎯 Design tradeoff
Why weld project_id into the ordering key instead of treating it as an ordinary filter column? Because that makes "isolation" and "efficiency" the same thing. If project_id were just a column, "see only my project" would be a full-table filter — slow at scale and dangerous (miss the filter once and you leak across tenants). Put it first in the ordering key and one project's data is physically adjacent, so "see only my project" degrades to "scan a contiguous range" — fast and structurally unable to see others. The cost: the ordering key is hard to change once set, and nearly every query must carry project_id (the repository layer's uniform contract). Make multi-tenant isolation part of the storage structure, not a filter you might forget.
🎯 Key points
  • Three nesting levels: organization (billing/members) ⊃ project (data boundary) ⊃ environment (prod/staging/dev within a project).
  • project = hard isolation (never visible across projects), environment = soft slice (a tag separable/combinable within a project).
  • project_id threads the whole pipeline: resolved from the API key → stamped on every row → leads the ClickHouse ordering key → locates queries. Isolation is welded into the ordering key, not an afterthought filter.
  • the environment column: LowCardinality(String) DEFAULT 'default' AFTER project_id (migration 0008, L08) — low-cardinality, backward-compatible, right after project_id.
  • Tradeoff: baking isolation into storage → isolation and efficiency become one, and it's structurally safe; the cost is a hard-to-change ordering key and project_id required on queries.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. In Langfuse multi-tenancy, what's the essential difference between project and environment?
  1. project is hard isolation (data never visible across projects, a security red line); environment is a soft slice (tagging prod/staging/dev within one project, separable or combinable)
  2. environment isolates more strongly than project
  3. They're identical, just different names
  4. project is soft, environment is hard
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. 'Different teams' data must never mix' is a security red line → project hard isolation; 'live vs test' is one team's view preference → environment soft slice. Different strengths, different treatment.
2. Why is Langfuse's tenant isolation 'not an afterthought WHERE project_id filter'?
  1. Because project_id is random
  2. Because each project uses a separate database instance
  3. Because Langfuse doesn't support filtering
  4. Because project_id is welded into the front of the ClickHouse ordering key: one project's data is physically adjacent, so 'see only my project' becomes scanning a contiguous range — isolation and efficiency in one, and structurally impossible to see others
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. If project_id were an ordinary filter column, it'd be slow at scale and dangerous (miss the filter and you leak across tenants). Put it first in the ordering key and isolation becomes 'locate a contiguous range' — fast and structurally safe. Cost: hard-to-change ordering key, project_id required on queries.
3. The ClickHouse environment column is LowCardinality(String) DEFAULT 'default' AFTER project_id. What's the intent of each?
  1. To remove project_id
  2. Pure placeholder, no intent
  3. To allow infinitely many env names
  4. Low-cardinality: few env names, saving space and speeding queries; default 'default': auto-classify when unspecified, backward-compatible with old data; right after project_id: forms a 'project+environment' locating prefix with it
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. LowCardinality suits low-distinct columns (compact encoding, fast queries); DEFAULT 'default' makes old/unspecified-env data fall into default (backward compatible); AFTER project_id forms a locating prefix so 'project+environment' queries stay efficient.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • 'Bake multi-tenant isolation into the storage structure, not a filter you might forget' — can you apply this to your own system? What benefits and constraints come from putting the tenant key first in the primary/ordering key?

Deployment topology

Part 2 covered what the data model and storage are. This lesson closes it with what it takes to run — to self-host Langfuse, which containers you actually start and how they connect. The answer is clean: 2 app containers + 4 infrastructure engines, all in the root docker-compose.yml. Land the abstract architecture of earlier lessons onto this concrete "packing list" and your mental model of Langfuse's "shape" is complete.

🔌 Analogy
Picture a Langfuse deployment as the rooms a restaurant needs: the front of house (web) greets guests, shows the menu, serves dishes; the kitchen (worker) quietly preps, washes up, handles big orders; the cold storage (ClickHouse) holds mountains of ingredients (telemetry); the filing cabinet (Postgres) keeps the menu and staff records (config); the order pass (Redis) queues tickets; the warehouse (S3/MinIO) stacks big, rarely-used stock (manuscripts, media). Miss a room and the restaurant limps — but assemble these 6 and you can open for business.
The self-host packing list: two app containers langfuse-web (langfuse/langfuse:3) and langfuse-worker (langfuse/langfuse-worker:3), plus four infra postgres:17 · clickhouse-server · redis:7 · minio (S3-compatible). Each infra gets a named volume for persistence. This is the "physical version" of the architecture in Lessons 5 and 7.

The minimal self-host stack: 2 apps + 4 infra

ServiceImageRole
langfuse-weblangfuse/langfuse:3user-facing: UI + tRPC + public REST API (ingestion entry)
langfuse-workerlangfuse/langfuse-worker:3background: consume queues, merge-write, eval, export…
postgrespostgres:17control plane: config/metadata (L09)
clickhouseclickhouse/clickhouse-serverdata plane: wide-event tables (L08)
redisredis:7BullMQ queues + cache
miniochainguard/minioS3-compatible object store (local stand-in): event log · media · exports

Note minio: an open-source object store that speaks the S3 protocol, standing in for AWS S3 locally / when self-hosting. So the same code uses real S3 in the cloud and MinIO when self-hosted, entirely transparent to the app layer — the portability of "program to an interface (the S3 protocol), not an implementation". The four infra containers each mount a named volume for persistence (postgres / clickhouse data+logs / redis / minio), while web and worker are stateless — no volumes; so docker compose down then up keeps your data.

app tierweb · worker
stateless business containers, restartable and scalable on demand. They start, connect to the storage below, and work.
data tierpostgres · clickhouse
two databases: control plane (config) and data plane (telemetry). Stateful, persisted via named volumes — the bedrock.
support tierredis · minio
queue/cache and object store. Make ingestion async and give big fields/media a home. Also stateful, also persisted.

These tiers have a plain startup order: bring up the data and support tiers and wait for them to be healthy, then start the app tier — so in compose the app containers depends_on the infra and wait for healthchecks. Grasp this layering and you have direction when debugging deployments: can't reach the DB → check the data tier; ingestion stalled → check Redis; media won't save → check MinIO.

web and worker: division and collaboration

The two app containers are Langfuse's "two hands". Similar in form (both connect the same storage) but opposite in duty:

Two hands: web is user-facing, low-latency, restartable; worker chews heavy jobs, retryable, scales by queue backlog. The two are decoupled by the Redis queue (L05), so you scale them separately — more users, add web; backed-up queue, add worker. This is the deployment-side expression of Lesson 4's "narrow waist" letting the two containers evolve independently.

scaling web

stateless, request-facing → watch concurrent users / QPS, run a few replicas behind a load balancer.

scaling worker

queue-facing → watch queue backlog, spin up more worker instances to consume (and shard by queue, L14).

Configuration is all environment variables

How do the six containers know each other's addresses, secrets and toggles? Environment variables. The repo has a set of .env.*.example templates (e.g. .env.dev.example, .env.prod.example) listing every variable to set (DB connections, S3 endpoint, encryption keys…). More importantly, these envs aren't read loosely — they pass through Zod validation (packages/shared/src/env.ts, worker/src/env.ts, etc.), checking types and required fields at startup. Misconfigure or miss one and the process fails to start and tells you exactly which variable is wrong, rather than running sick and crashing midway. This "validate config at startup" discipline turns "config errors" from hard-to-find runtime bugs into obvious startup errors (expanded in L51).

Put it together and the first self-host experience is actually simple: clone the repo → copy an .env.dev.example and fill the key variables → docker compose up, and the six containers come up in dependency order, connect, and within minutes you open a browser to the Langfuse UI. The repo also ships several compose files for different scenarios — docker-compose.dev.yml (local dev, often just the infra while the apps run on your machine for hot reload), docker-compose.yml (the full stack), and cloud-specific variants (e.g. Azure). This "one orchestration for the whole stack, variants per scenario" is exactly how the earlier "four-dependency complexity" gets folded into a few yml files — you needn't hand-install each database or wire the network; compose strings it all together. Grasp this topology and you'll not only "get it running" but also know which container's logs to check when something breaks.

🎯 Design tradeoff
Are four infra dependencies a burden for self-hosters? Honestly, yes — versus "one binary + one SQLite", Langfuse's self-host bar is genuinely higher. But that's the deployment-side consequence of Lesson 7's tradeoff: to handle production scale (massive telemetry, async ingestion, object storage), none of the four is droppable. Langfuse's mitigation is bringing up all six containers with one docker-compose command and using a MinIO stand-in to remove the dependency on cloud S3, making "run the whole thing on your own machine" easy. Complexity can't be erased, but it can be packaged and delivered in one command. That's why the docs say "self-host in minutes" — not no dependencies, but dependencies orchestrated for you.
🎯 Key points
  • Minimal self-host stack = 2 apps + 4 infra: langfuse-web + langfuse-worker, plus postgres · clickhouse · redis · minio(S3), all in docker-compose.yml.
  • web vs worker: same storage, opposite duties — web user-facing/low-latency, scales by QPS; worker heavy/retryable, scales by queue backlog; decoupled by the Redis queue.
  • MinIO is an S3-protocol stand-in: same code uses real S3 in the cloud, MinIO locally — transparent to the app.
  • Config is all env vars, Zod-validated at startup (env.ts) — misconfigure and it fails to start with a clear message, no sick running.
  • Four deps are the necessary cost of scale (L07), mitigated by one-command docker-compose orchestration — "plenty of deps, but packaged".

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What containers make up the minimal stack to self-host Langfuse?
  1. Only ClickHouse
  2. web + worker + one SQLite file
  3. Just one langfuse container
  4. 2 apps (langfuse-web, langfuse-worker) + 4 infra (postgres, clickhouse, redis, minio/S3), all in docker-compose.yml
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. docker-compose.yml lists two app containers (web/worker) plus four infra (postgres:17, clickhouse-server, redis:7, minio). It's the physical version of the architecture in L05/L07.
2. web and worker connect the same storage — why split into two containers, and why scale them separately?
  1. Because one container can't hold the code
  2. Opposite duties: web is user-facing, low-latency, scales by QPS; worker chews heavy jobs, retryable, scales by queue backlog. Decoupled by the Redis queue, so each scales independently without blocking the other
  3. Because they use different databases
  4. Just to run more machines
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. User pressure (QPS) and background pressure (queue backlog) are independent scaling signals. Splitting into two containers decoupled by a queue lets you 'add web for more users, add worker for a backed-up queue', without entanglement. It's the deployment-side expression of L04's narrow waist.
3. Langfuse validates env vars with Zod 'at startup'. What's the benefit of this discipline?
  1. To make startup slower for checking
  2. To auto-fix wrong config
  3. To encrypt env vars
  4. A misconfig/missing var makes the process fail to start and names the bad variable — turning 'config errors' from hard-to-find runtime bugs into obvious startup errors, instead of running sick and crashing midway
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Fail-fast: env.ts uses Zod to validate types and required fields at startup, reporting clearly on error. Far more debuggable than 'running with bad config and crashing in some corner feature later' (expanded in L51).
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Langfuse self-host needs 4 infra engines, a higher bar than 'one binary'. If you built an open-source tool, how would you trade off 'handles scale' vs 'easy to self-host'? Is one-command docker-compose orchestration a good answer?

The ingestion API

Part 2 finished "what the data looks like and where it lives". From here we walk the ingestion path — the close-up of Lesson 5's "write trunk". First stop: the public ingestion API — how the server receives a batch of events your app sends. Spoiler on its character: extremely fast and light. It does just three things — auth + validate + enqueue — then returns immediately, never merging, computing cost, or writing the DB here (those are the worker's job). This is Lesson 5's "fast lane".

🔌 Analogy
Picture the ingestion API as a courier's drop-off counter: the courier (your SDK) brings a batch of parcels (events), and the clerk (the API) does three things before waving you through — check the sender's identity (auth: which project is this key?), scan and log the labels (validate: is each parcel well-formed?), drop them on the belt (enqueue). Then it hands you a receipt on the spot (HTTP 200) and lets you go — it won't sort, weigh and deliver in front of you. The slow work is done by people down the belt (the worker). The counter's only goal: accept fast, never block the door.
A minimal accept flow: auth (API key resolves a project, L09·10) → Zod-validate event shapes → processEventBatch enqueues (and lands originals in S3) → immediate 200. No "heavy work" happens here, so the API always returns in milliseconds.

The life of one ingestion request

The entry is the handler in web/src/pages/api/public/ingestion.ts. After taking the body, the real work is a shared function called processEventBatch:

web/src/pages/api/public/ingestion.tshandler
import { processEventBatch } from "@langfuse/shared/src/server";

export default async function handler(req, res) {
  // …auth, parse body…
  const result = await processEventBatch(batchForProcessing, authCheck);
  // return the result immediately (success / partial failure), not waiting for the worker
}

One design detail: processEventBatch is shared — besides /ingestion, even the standalone "submit a score" endpoint reuses it. Why? Because "accept a batch, validate, enqueue" is needed everywhere; factoring it into one function avoids re-implementing (and re-bugging) it per endpoint (again Lesson 6's "converge complexity to one place"). The event types it supports cover TRACE_CREATE / OBSERVATION_CREATE / SPAN_CREATE / GENERATION_CREATE / SCORE_CREATE and more (including SDK_LOG, DATASET_RUN_ITEM_CREATE) — exactly Lesson 6's event envelope.

Why a "batch", not "one"

The SDK doesn't fire one HTTP per event — it collects a batch and sends it. This "batch" design saves a lot:

😖 one-by-one

Every observation a full HTTP round trip; at high frequency the network overhead is huge and connections explode — a real drag on your app.

😀 batched

Dozens or hundreds of events in one request, one round trip. Network overhead amortizes, and the API validates and enqueues in one go — far higher throughput.

Batching also gives a semantic benefit: a batch may hold both a CREATE and an UPDATE for one observation, and the server can process them together, cutting round trips. Of course a batch can't be infinite — when the body is too large the API rejects it ("payload too large"), another guard: don't let one request blow up the server.

1

SDK batches → POST /ingestion

the app packs a window of events into one batch and sends it.

2

handler authenticates

resolve the project from the API key and check write permission (fail → reject the whole batch).

3

Zod-validate each event

ill-formed events are rejected; the rest continue (partial success possible).

4

processEventBatch enqueues + lands S3

valid events enter the Redis queue; originals are stored in S3 (L14).

5

return the result immediately

tell the caller which were accepted, which rejected — all in milliseconds, not waiting for the worker.

Two gates: auth + validation

Before "enqueue", the API must pass two gates. They look routine but are the system's first line of security and robustness:

Two gates: auth resolves the project from the API key (L09: a key belongs to a project) and confirms write permission — this also pins the tenant (L10's project_id threading); Zod validation checks each event's shape, so bad data is rejected at the door and never pollutes downstream. Pass both, then enqueue.

The second gate, Zod validation, especially matters: the ingestion system swallows a flood of data from many SDKs and versions daily, inevitably with malformed or missing-field "dirty events". Rejecting them at the entry with a Zod schema and a clear error beats letting them flow into the queue and crash the worker — the earlier you reject bad data, the calmer the whole system. If a few events in a batch are bad, the API can still return "partial success", telling you which weren't accepted, rather than failing the whole batch.

This gateGuardsOn failure
authIs the API key valid? Which project? May it write?401/403, whole batch rejected
Zod validationIs each event's shape/type legalbad events rejected; partial success possible
size guardIs the body too large"payload too large", prevents blow-up

"Partial success" isn't just talk — it's baked into the response contract: the last line of ingestion.ts's handler is res.status(207).json(result), where result looks like { successes, errors } — two arrays listing the accepted events and the rejected ones (each with its error reason). The 207 Multi-Status HTTP code is purpose-built for "one request, many sub-operations, mixed outcomes". So even if 3 of your 100 events are dirty, the server accepts 97, returns the 3 with reasons, instead of throwing out the whole pot for a few bad apples. Hugely SDK-friendly: the client can retransmit just the failed ones, no full-batch retry.

🎯 Design tradeoff
Why does the API only "auth + validate + enqueue", pushing all heavy work to the worker? Because the ingestion API is the layer facing your app directly, and its top KPI is "always fast, never drag the caller". Any extra work here — merging, looking up pricing, writing the DB — becomes latency on every instrumentation call and can fail on downstream hiccups. So Langfuse cuts this line very cleanly: the API keeps only the minimum that must happen on the spot (auth to prevent unauthorized writes, validation to keep dirty data out, enqueue to hand off), and everything else that can be async is async. The cost is "eventual consistency" (L05), but the gain is an ingestion entry that's almost impossible to slow or to drag your business down. The thinner the entry, the better it survives spikes.
🎯 Key points
  • The ingestion API's entire job: auth + Zod validation + enqueue, then return immediately — merge/cost/DB-write are not here (the worker's job).
  • The handler in ingestion.ts calls the shared processEventBatch (even the standalone score endpoint reuses it).
  • Batched reporting: dozens/hundreds of events per HTTP, amortizing network cost, validated and enqueued in one go; oversized batches are rejected (size guard).
  • Two gates: auth (key→project, permission, pins the tenant) + Zod validation (reject bad data at the door, partial success possible).
  • Tradeoff: the entry keeps only "must happen now", everything async-able is async — the thinner the entry the better it survives spikes, at the cost of eventual consistency.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. The ingestion API does only three things on a batch, then returns immediately. Which three, and why not merge/cost/write-DB here?
  1. auth + merge + write-DB; because it's fastest
  2. auth + Zod validation + enqueue; because the API faces your app directly, its top goal is 'always fast, never drag the caller'; heavy work here would add latency to every call and fail on downstream hiccups
  3. compress + encrypt + enqueue
  4. only enqueue, no auth or validation
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The entry keeps only the must-do-now minimum (auth prevents unauthorized writes, validation blocks dirty data, enqueue hands off), deferring everything async to the worker. The thinner the entry, the better it survives spikes and the less it drags your business — at the cost of eventual consistency (L05).
2. The ingestion.ts handler delegates the real work to a shared processEventBatch, reused even by the standalone score endpoint. The benefit?
  1. to make the score endpoint slower
  2. 'accept a batch, validate, enqueue' is needed everywhere; factoring it into one place avoids re-implementing (and re-bugging) it per endpoint (converge complexity, L06)
  3. just to write more code
  4. because scores can't use the queue
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Multiple endpoints share one ingestion routine, maintained and tested in one place, avoiding inconsistency from scattered implementations — in line with L06's 'converge complexity to one strictly testable place'.
3. Why 'validate with Zod at the entry, reject bad data as early as possible', and why can the API return 'partial success'?
  1. Ingestion swallows data from many SDKs/versions daily, inevitably with dirty events; rejecting at the entry with a clear error beats letting them crash the worker; a few bad events in a batch get rejected while the rest are accepted
  2. because bad data is faster
  3. because partial success saves money
  4. because Zod encrypts data
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Fail-fast + fault tolerance: a schema blocks dirty data at the door, sparing downstream and the worker; per-event partial success avoids letting a few bad events doom the whole batch. This keeps ingestion from massive heterogeneous sources both robust and friendly.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Splitting 'accept' from 'process' so the entry does the minimum — can you apply this to your own APIs? Which work 'must happen now', and which can be deferred to async background? What if you split it wrong?

Event types & merge semantics

Last lesson the API accepted a batch of events and enqueued them. Now we zoom into a single event: what does it look like? Why does it have two faces, create and update? And most crucially — how do the several events your SDK sends end up as one row in the database? This is the soul of Langfuse ingestion: an event stream merges into a record. Get this and Lesson 8's ReplacingMergeTree(event_ts, is_deleted) design finally clicks into place.

🩺 Analogy
Think of one observation as a hospital chart. When the patient checks in, the doctor writes the first visit note (generation-create: start time, the input complaint). After tests and a prescription, the doctor adds a note (generation-update: end time, diagnosis, dosage). Both notes carry the same patient number (entity id) — so when filing, the nurse doesn't create two charts, she merges them into one, latest note winning on conflicts. Each note also has its own serial number (event id), so filing the same note twice never duplicates it. "Merge by patient number, dedup by serial" — exactly the two ids this lesson dissects.

The envelope: id · timestamp · type · body

Every ingestion event — whether it describes a trace, an observation or a score — rides in the same envelope: a shared "shell" plus a type-dependent payload body. Langfuse pins this down with Zod: a base (the shell), each type extends it with its own type and body, and finally a discriminated union (z.discriminatedUnion("type", […])) gathers the dozen-plus events into one schema. To validate, Zod reads the type field and knows which body rules to apply — "one field sets the identity, one ruleset per identity".

Envelope = fixed shell + variable body: base holds id (event id), timestamp, metadata and the discriminator type; type decides which Zod ruleset the body follows. That's exactly how z.discriminatedUnion("type", […]) works. Source: packages/shared/src/server/ingestion/types.ts:597.
packages/shared/src/server/ingestion/types.tsL597
const base = z.object({
  id: idSchema,                        // event id (serial)
  timestamp: z.iso.datetime({ offset: true }),
  metadata: jsonSchema.nullish(),
});

const generationCreateEvent = base.extend({
  type: z.literal(eventTypes.GENERATION_CREATE),
  body: CreateGenerationBody,          // payload varies by type
});
// …span-create / span-update / score-create / 18 in total…

const ingestionEvent = z.discriminatedUnion("type", [
  traceEvent, scoreEvent, spanCreateEvent, spanUpdateEvent,
  generationCreateEvent, generationUpdateEvent, /* … */
]);

The beauty of the discriminated union: it isn't "make every field optional, fill in whatever" — it's a strict body ruleset per type. Send generation-create and you must match CreateGenerationBody; send score-create and you must match ScoreBody — wrong type or missing field, and Lesson 12's Zod gate rejects it on the spot. The schema even ships as two instances, public and internal: things like dataset-run-item-create are internal-only and rejected on the public API.

The body "matryoshka": Event ⊂ Span ⊂ Generation

Those body schemas aren't written separately — they inherit layer by layer. The innermost is OptionalObservationBody (traceId, name, startTime, input/output…); extend it with id to get CreateEventEvent, add endTime for CreateSpanBody, then add model / usage / modelParameters for CreateGenerationBody. This chain maps precisely onto Lesson 3's three observation kinds: EVENT is a point in time (no duration), SPAN has start & end (adds endTime), GENERATION is a SPAN with LLM fields (adds model/usage). Tellingly, the newer observation types from Lesson 8 — AGENT / TOOL / CHAIN / RETRIEVER / EVALUATOR / EMBEDDING / GUARDRAIL — all reuse CreateGenerationBody: at heart they're each "a generation-rich span", differing only in semantic label.

The further out, the more capable: EVENT is a duration-less point → SPAN adds endTime and gains start/end → GENERATION adds model/usage, becoming an LLM call that records tokens and cost. The 7 newer types (AGENT/TOOL/CHAIN…) reuse CreateGenerationBody directly. Source: types.ts:421-470.

This inheritance doesn't just save code, it makes the type semantics escalate step by step: you can't put an endTime on an EVENT (its body simply has no such field), nor a usage on a SPAN. The schema is the documentation — read the chain and you see at a glance what each of the three observation kinds can and cannot carry.

The heart of it: several events, one row

Now to answer the opening question. For one LLM call, the SDK usually sends two events: generation-create at the start (with startTime, input) and generation-update at the end (with endTime, output, usage). Yet they land as the same observation row in ClickHouse. The secret is two ids, each with its own job:

entity idbody.id (merge key)
The id of the observation/trace itself. All events with the same body.id are destined to merge into one row. create and update share it, so they point at the same record.
event idenvelope base.id (dedup key)
Each event's own serial. In S3 it becomes the filename <eventId>.json — resend the same event, overwrite the same file, naturally idempotent, never counted twice.
versiontimestamp (who wins)
When merging conflicting fields, the later-timestamped event wins (last-write-wins). This is the write-side mirror of Lesson 8's ReplacingMergeTree(event_ts).
create + update → one row: the two events share body.id=obs-42, get grouped and merged; each field takes the side that has a value, conflicts resolved by later timestamp. The worker's merge logic (Lesson 15) is a "write-side rehearsal" of Lesson 8's ReplacingMergeTree behavior.

Break this "event stream → one row" path into five steps and the whole merge story is clear — the first two happen on your app side, the last three on the Langfuse server:

1

Start: send create

As the LLM call begins, the SDK sends generation-create with body.id, startTime, input. Fire-and-forget, nothing held in memory.

2

End: send update

When the call ends, the SDK sends generation-update, reusing the same body.id, adding endTime, output, usage.

3

Group by body.id

The server groups events with the same dedupKey = entityType-body.id — these two are recognized as "two reports of one record".

4

Merge fields (latest wins)

Take the set side of each field; where both create and update gave a value, the later timestamp wins.

5

Land as one row

The merged result writes into one row of observations (id=body.id); ReplacingMergeTree re-dedups in the background as a safety net (Lesson 8).

18 event types, 5 entities, 3 core tables

Event type comes in many flavors, but when the worker writes, it cares about one thing: which table does this go to. A small function getClickhouseEntityType collapses the 18 types into 5 entity types (trace / observation / score / sdk_log / dataset_run_item); of these, the three core ones — trace / observation / score — map exactly onto Lesson 8's three ReplacingMergeTree wide tables, while the other two (SDK diagnostic sdk_log, dataset-run linkage dataset_run_item) take their own handling:

entity typemapped from which event typeswhich table
tracetrace-createtraces (Lesson 8)
observationevent/span/generation-create·update + agent/tool/chain/retriever/evaluator/embedding/guardrail-create + legacy observation-*observations (Lesson 8)
scorescore-createscores (Lesson 8)
sdk_logsdk-logSDK diagnostics, handled separately
dataset_run_itemdataset-run-item-createdataset run linkage, handled separately

Within the three core types, everything "observation-like" — be it span, generation or agent — lands in observation, the same table, distinguished by the type column on the observation row. That's the power of Lesson 8's "wide events": one table holds every observation shape, and adding a new observation kind needs no new table, no schema change.

packages/shared/src/server/ingestion/processEventBatch.tsgrouping
// Group events by eventBodyId so multiple events of "one entity" store & process together
const entityType = getClickhouseEntityType(event.type);  // → trace / observation / score
const dedupKey = `${entityType}-${event.body.id}`;       // merge key = entity type + entity id

// each event lands in S3 named by its "event id": <eventId>.json (resend → overwrite, idempotent)
key: safeBlobFilenameStem(event.id, ".json"),

Read this snippet and the whole picture lands: the entity id (body.id) decides "which row to merge into", the event id (envelope id) decides "what it's named in S3". The former lets create/update converge into one record; the latter ensures resending the same event a hundred times still counts once. Two ids, two purposes, fitting together seamlessly.

🎯 Design tradeoff
Why not just do an upsert — why split into two create / update events? Because the SDK shouldn't carry state. An LLM call can take seconds, even tens of seconds, between start and end; meanwhile the app might crash or move machines. Requiring "wait until the end, then upsert the whole thing" forces the SDK to hold that record in memory until completion — costing memory and risking loss. Splitting into create + update lets the SDK send half at the start, the rest at the end, forgetting each as it goes; the dirty work of merging by body.id falls to the server. Pushing state from the client to the center is the classic distributed-systems move — "keep the edge stateless, let the center backstop it" — echoing Lesson 5's fast-lane / slow-lane split.
🎯 Key points
  • Each event = an envelope (base: id · timestamp · metadata + discriminator type) + a type-varying body, gathered by z.discriminatedUnion("type") into one schema.
  • body is an inheritance chain: OptionalObservationBody → +id CreateEventEvent → +endTime CreateSpanBody → +model/usage CreateGenerationBody, mapping EVENT⊂SPAN⊂GENERATION.
  • Two ids: entity id (body.id) is the merge key — same-id create/update converge into one row; event id (envelope id) is the dedup key — becomes the S3 filename, guaranteeing idempotency.
  • Conflicting fields resolve by later timestamp (last-write-wins), the write-side mirror of Lesson 8's ReplacingMergeTree(event_ts).
  • getClickhouseEntityType collapses the 18 event types into 5 entity types; the three core ones trace / observation / score → Lesson 8's three wide tables (the other two, sdk_log & dataset_run_item, handled separately).

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. For one LLM call the SDK sends generation-create then generation-update, yet they land as the same row in ClickHouse. How?
  1. Both events share one entity id (body.id); the server groups by body.id and merges into one row, conflicts resolved by later timestamp (last-write-wins)
  2. they merge because their event ids are equal
  3. ClickHouse randomly keeps one of them
  4. the SDK merges locally and sends just one
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. The entity id (body.id) is the merge key: same-id create/update converge into one row; the event id (envelope id) is the dedup key, becoming the S3 filename .json for idempotency. Conflicts resolve latest-timestamp-wins — the write-side mirror of L08's ReplacingMergeTree(event_ts).
2. Events are defined with z.discriminatedUnion("type", […]) rather than 'one big schema with all fields optional'. The core benefit?
  1. A strict body ruleset per type: generation-create must match CreateGenerationBody; missing/wrong fields are rejected by Zod on the spot — precise validation with clear errors
  2. makes events smaller on the wire
  3. lets you drop the type field
  4. lets every event fill any field freely
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. The discriminated union uses the single type field to pick the body ruleset — 'one field sets identity, one ruleset per identity'. Far more precise than 'all-optional', which can't catch structural errors at the entry and lets dirty data crash the worker downstream.
3. getClickhouseEntityType collapses 18 event types into 5 entity types; where do span/generation/agent/tool land among them, and what does that imply?
  1. all into observation, the same observations table, distinguished by the row's type column; adding a new observation kind needs no new table or schema change — the power of L08's 'wide events'
  2. each into its own separate table
  3. into the score table
  4. into the trace table
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Among the 5 entity types, the three core ones trace / observation / score map onto L08's three ReplacingMergeTree wide tables (the other two, sdk_log & dataset_run_item, are handled separately). Every 'observation-like' shape shares the one observations table, distinguished by the type column, so Langfuse can keep adding observation kinds (AGENT/TOOL/CHAIN…) without schema migrations.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Splitting create/update into two events pushes the 'hold the whole record' state from client to server. In your own systems, is there a similar 'keep the edge stateless, let the center backstop' spot? What are its costs and benefits?

The ingestion queue

Lesson 12 had the API "enqueue" and vanish; Lesson 13 explained what "event" it enqueues. The star of this lesson is the queue itself — a conveyor belt built on Redis + BullMQ. It's the handoff point between Lesson 5's fast lane and slow lane: web drops the work and leaves, while the worker pulls it off the other end and processes at its own pace. Three questions to answer: what's actually in the queue (spoiler: not the event itself, but a "claim ticket"), why split into a primary / secondary queue, and how sharding survives spikes under high concurrency.

🛄 Analogy
Think of the queue as an airport baggage belt. The check-in desk (API) doesn't pile your suitcase on the counter — it sends the case to the back room (S3) and puts only a baggage tag (a pointer: the case's id in the back room) on the belt. A handler (worker) takes the tag off the belt, fetches the case by its id, then processes it. The beauty: the belt (Redis memory) only carries featherweight tags, fast and cheap; the bulky cases (event bodies, possibly large) rest quietly in the back. If handlers fall behind, tags simply queue on the belt and not a single case is lost — because the back room is the goods' "real ledger".

The queue holds a "claim ticket", not the event body

Here's the most-misunderstood point of the whole ingestion path: the Redis queue does not store the event's full content. Before enqueuing, Lesson 12's processEventBatch first writes each event to S3 named <eventId>.json (Lesson 13's event id becomes the filename); the queue holds only a lightweight job whose key field is a fileKey — a pointer to that S3 file. The worker takes the job, then reads the event back from S3 by fileKey.

Pointer and body, separated: the event body is written to S3 (<eventId>.json); the queue holds only a lightweight job carrying fileKey. The worker reads the body back from S3 by fileKey. Source: processEventBatch.ts:340-398.
packages/shared/src/server/ingestion/processEventBatch.tsenqueue
const shardingKey = `${projectId}-${eventData.eventBodyId}`;   // shard key (below)
const queue = IngestionQueue.getInstance({ shardingKey });

await queue.add(QueueJobs.IngestionJob, {
  id: randomUUID(), timestamp: new Date(),
  payload: {
    data: { type, eventBodyId, fileKey: eventData.key, /* points to S3 */ },
    authCheck,                          // carry the tenant identity too (Lesson 10)
  },
}, { delay });

📨 the queue holds a pointer

The payload is a tiny { type, eventBodyId, fileKey, authCheck } job of a few dozen bytes. Redis memory is precious; the smaller the job, the faster the enqueue and the better it holds up under backlog.

📦 S3 holds the body

The raw event (input/output can be large) sits in S3. It's also the historical event source Lesson 15's merge reads back, and the basis for rebuilding should a job ever be lost.

One more detail hides in the enqueue: queue.add(…, { delay }) attaches a small delay (about 5s by default). Why be deliberately "a beat slow"? Because the create and update for one entity may arrive back to back; if a worker processes the create before the update lands in S3, duplicates or out-of-order writes can result. The delay lets a batch of related events "settle" first, so the worker takes them together, naturally reducing duplicate processing. Better still, getDelay lengthens the delay around the UTC 23:45–00:15 "date boundary" — because Lesson 8's ordering key contains toDate(start_time), events straddling midnight, if processed too eagerly, could split across two date partitions and duplicate. This unassuming delay is exactly what safeguards Lesson 8's day-organized storage — a classic "trade a little latency for consistency".

Sharding: split one queue into N, survive the spike

A single Redis queue always has a ceiling. When a deployment enables Redis cluster (REDIS_CLUSTER_ENABLED=true), the ingestion queue is sharded into several: ingestion-queue, ingestion-queue-1, ingestion-queue-2… the count set by LANGFUSE_INGESTION_QUEUE_SHARD_COUNT. Which shard depends on the hash of the shard key projectId-eventBodyId: SHA-256 produces a number, taken modulo the shard count to yield a shard index.

Shard without scattering an entity: the hash is over projectId-eventBodyId, so all events of one entity (create/update) necessarily land on the same shard — spreading load across Redis nodes while keeping a record's multiple reports ordered on one worker. Source: redis/ingestionQueue.ts:38-52, redis/sharding.ts:9.

Choosing projectId-eventBodyId as the shard key — rather than a random number — is deliberate: random sharding would scatter one record's create and update across different shards, forcing cross-queue coordination at merge time, complex and prone to disorder. Using the entity id as the shard key makes "same entity, same shard" hold naturally — the same idea as Lesson 8 leading the ordering key with project_id and Lesson 13 using body.id as the merge key: keep related data physically together.

Primary vs secondary: don't let a noisy tenant block everyone
