2. A monitor stays in ALERT. It computes every 5 minutes but does not send an alert every 5 minutes. What implements this restraint, and what problem does it solve?
  1. applyStateMachine: as a rule emit only on a severity 'change' (OK→ALERT sends, ALERT→ALERT doesn't), topping up persistent anomalies per the renotify period — curing alert fatigue
  2. it sends every time, relying on Slack to dedupe
  3. it randomly drops some alerts
  4. it only sends during work hours
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Alert fatigue is a monitoring system's top killer: a persistent ALERT pinging every 5 minutes makes engineers mute the channel and bury real issues. applyStateMachine compares prev/next severity, considering emit only when severityChanged, and tops up per renotify; PAUSED is skipped to not overwrite user intent. Making severity a state machine rather than an instantaneous value is exactly to ask 'did it change from last time'.
3. After deciding to alert, a monitor doesn't call the Slack API directly but publishes the alert to the WebhookQueue, with the automation system forwarding it. Why this design?
  1. decision separate from delivery (decoupling): the monitor only decides 'whether to alert' and needn't know each delivery channel; delivery can evolve/retry/add channels independently with the monitor unchanged — echoing Lesson 30's create/execute split
  2. Slack can't be called directly
  3. to archive the alerts
  4. the WebhookQueue is faster than calling Slack directly
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. A monitor's job is deciding whether a line is crossed, not coupling to 'how the alert reaches a human' (Slack/webhook/email/on-call). Publishing to one WebhookQueue, with automation (Lesson 44's Trigger→Action) choosing the channel, lets delivery evolve/retry/add channels independently while the monitor stays put. Same decoupling taste as Lesson 30's create/execute split and Lesson 12's one-entry-many-producers.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Part 5 is now complete: the score model (28) → four ways to produce scores (29–32) → active monitoring and alerting (33). Reviewing the whole part, Langfuse turns 'evaluation' from a static 'can score, can query' system into a dynamic closed loop that 'auto-evaluates, alerts actively, and calibrates AI with human scores'. If you designed an evaluation system for your own LLM app, how would you combine these five capabilities (human/LLM-judge/code/monitoring)? Which quality signals would you continuously monitor and alert on, and why?

Datasets & items

Evaluation needs "exam questions". To know whether your app got better or worse after you changed a prompt or swapped a model, you need a fixed set of test cases to quiz it on repeatedly. That set is a dataset; each question in it is a dataset item: one input + one expected output. This lesson covers the data model of datasets and items, focusing on two delightful designs: an item can be "promoted" from a real production trace into a test case (a hard case you hit in production becomes a regression test), and items are versioned—editing a question keeps a version of history, so "which version of the question did last experiment use" is always answerable.

📋 Analogy
A dataset is like a standard exam paper. Each question (item) has a prompt (input) and a reference answer (expectedOutput). Questions come two ways: you can write one, or you can capture verbatim a real, botched case from production (a trace) into the paper—a "production incident" thus becomes a "regression question". The most careful part is the edit rule: when you revise a question, the system does not erase the old version—it stamps the old one with a "valid-until" and starts a new version. So every version of the question is kept, and "which version of the prompt did the March exam use" is always retrievable—lifeblood for "experiments must be reproducible".

A dataset = a set of test cases, and questions can be "picked" from production

The data model is intuitive: a Dataset holds a set of DatasetItems. A Dataset has a name, a description, and can attach inputSchema / expectedOutputSchema to give questions a format spec. Each DatasetItem's core is three things: input (the prompt), expectedOutput (the reference answer), metadata (side notes). But the truly clever fields are two others: sourceTraceId and sourceObservationId—they record "which real trace this question came from".

Where questions come from: schema.prisma:685's DatasetItem has input/expectedOutput/metadata, plus sourceTraceId/sourceObservationId—the latter let you promote a real trace (Lesson 13) into a test case. A Dataset (:661) can also constrain question format via inputSchema/expectedOutputSchema.
prompt + answerinput / expectedOutput
An item's body: input is fed to the app, expectedOutput is the reference answer you expect. Evaluation compares the app's real output against expectedOutput (or hands it to Lessons 29–31's judges). metadata holds tags, difficulty, and other notes.
from productionsourceTraceId / sourceObservationId
The dataset's most vital source: spotting a real botched case in a trace detail, you capture it in one click as an item, with sourceTraceId recording its origin. So your test set hugs the real traffic distribution, not imagined toy questions.
statusDatasetStatus
An item is ACTIVE / ARCHIVED: archiving doesn't delete—it just retires the item from the test set, keeping history and links. The dataset itself can attach a schema (inputSchema/expectedOutputSchema) to set rules for new questions.

A built dataset can do three big things—it's the foundation for the next two lessons of Part 6:

regression testing

After changing a prompt / swapping a model, re-run the whole set and check whether scores dropped—preventing "fix one bug, break three cases".

experiment comparison

The same set, run once per config (Lessons 35–36's runs), with multiple results placed side by side to pick the best combination.

few-shot example bank

An item's input/expectedOutput can also serve as examples in a prompt (Lesson 37), letting the model answer in the shape of the reference answers.

Versioning: edit a question by closing the old version, opening a new

An item's most refined design is versioning. The primary key says it all: @@id([id, projectId, validFrom])—the same question (same id) can have multiple rows, distinguished by validFrom. Each row also has a validTo: the row where it's null is the currently active version. On edit, the system does two steps in a transaction—stamp the old version with validTo (close its validity interval), then insert a new version row. This is the database's classic "slowly-changing dimension / bitemporal" technique.

Bitemporal versions: the current version = the row with validTo IS NULL. An edit, in a transaction, "closes the old version (stamps validTo) + inserts a new version (new validFrom)". A dataset run pins a validFrom, so it always sees "that version of the question"—exactly why Lesson 30's eval uses datasetItemValidFrom. Source: dataset-items.ts:418-470.
packages/shared/src/server/repositories/dataset-items.ts:418-470VERSIONED write
// in one transaction: close the old version, then insert the new (SCD Type 2)
await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
  // 0. find the current version (the row with validTo null)
  const current = await tx.datasetItem.findFirst({
    where: { id: itemId, projectId, validTo: null }, orderBy: { validFrom: "desc" } });
  const newValidFrom = new Date(Math.max(Date.now(), baseTs + 1));

  // 1. stamp the old version with validTo — close its validity interval
  if (current) await tx.datasetItem.update({ where: {…current.validFrom}, data: { validTo: newValidFrom } });

  // 2. insert the new version — validFrom = that moment, validTo defaults null (the new current)
  await tx.datasetItem.create({ data: { ...itemData, validFrom: newValidFrom } });
});

Why go to all this trouble instead of editing in place? Because experiments must be reproducible. You ran an experiment in March and got scores; in June you tweaked a few questions and ran again. If questions were "edited in place", March's scores would lose their context—you wouldn't know which version they tested, and the two experiments couldn't be compared fairly. Versioning pins every version of every question on a timeline, so "March's run used version t2, June's used t3" is crystal clear, and historical conclusions stay forever explainable.

id (logical question)validFromvalidTocurrent?content
item-42t1t2no (history)prompt v1
item-42t2t3no (history)prompt v2
item-42t3nullyes ✓prompt v3 (current)

Three rows with the same id, different validFrom, distinguished as "current vs history" by whether validTo is null. To ask "what does this question look like now", WHERE validTo IS NULL; to ask "what did the t2 experiment use", find the row where validFrom ≤ t2 < validTo—one table answers both "now" and "any historical moment", the power of bitemporal.

🎯 Design trade-off
Why version dataset items rather than simply update in place? In a phrase: to keep historical experiments forever explainable and reproducible. The whole value of evaluation is "comparison"—is this better or worse than last time. But if the test questions are silently edited, "last time"'s score becomes rootless: you don't know what it tested, and can't fairly compare with "this time". Versioning uses validFrom/validTo to pin a time interval on every version of every question, letting each run pin the version it used (Lesson 30's eval replays exactly via datasetItemValidFrom). It's the classic "immutable history + a movable current pointer", the same belief as Lesson 37's prompt versions and git commits: once truth is recorded it's not erased; to change, append a new version.

Why let items be "promoted" from real traces? Because the best test questions hide in production incidents. Imagined test cases rarely cover the tricky, long-tail inputs real users throw; whereas every botched production trace is a ready-made, real-distribution question. sourceTraceId closes the loop "find a problem → capture it as a regression question → verify after improving"—an observability platform not only lets you see problems but turns them into "vaccines" against recurrence.
🎯 Key points
  • A dataset = a set of test cases: a Dataset holds DatasetItems, each = input (prompt) + expectedOutput (reference answer) + metadata. It's the "exam paper" for evaluation, used with Lessons 28–32's scores.
  • Questions can be "promoted" from production: sourceTraceId/sourceObservationId let you capture a real trace (Lesson 13) into a test case in one click—the regression set hugs the real traffic distribution, not made-up cases.
  • Items are versioned: the key (id, projectId, validFrom) lets many versions of one question coexist; the current = validTo IS NULL. An edit, in a transaction, "closes the old (stamps validTo) + inserts the new"—slowly-changing dimension / bitemporal.
  • Versioning is for reproducibility: a run pins a validFrom, always seeing "that version" (Lesson 30's eval replays via datasetItemValidFrom). Historical experiments stay explainable and fairly comparable.
  • Immutable history + a movable pointer: the same belief as Lesson 37's prompt versions and git commits—record truth without erasing it; append a new version to change.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Langfuse's DatasetItem has sourceTraceId / sourceObservationId fields. What unique capability do they give datasets?
  1. storing items in ClickHouse
  2. encrypting items
  3. promoting a real production trace into a test case: spot a botched hard case in production, capture it as a regression question in one click, keeping the test set close to the real traffic distribution rather than imagined
  4. auto-scoring dataset items
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. sourceTraceId/sourceObservationId record which real trace a question came from. Imagined test cases miss real long-tail inputs, while every botched production trace is a ready-made question. This closes the loop 'find a problem → capture as regression question → verify after improving'—the platform turns production problems into vaccines against recurrence.
2. A DatasetItem's primary key is (id, projectId, validFrom), and each row has a validTo. When you revise a question, what does the system do?
  1. only changes the updatedAt timestamp
  2. in a transaction: stamp the old version with validTo (close its validity interval), then insert a new version row (new validFrom, validTo null becoming current)—slowly-changing dimension / bitemporal, not erasing history
  3. deletes the old row then creates a new one
  4. updates that row in place
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. dataset-items.ts VERSIONED write: in a transaction, find the current version (validTo IS NULL), stamp it with validTo=newValidFrom to close its interval, then create a new row with validFrom=newValidFrom (validTo defaults null). So many versions of one question coexist, the current being the validTo-null row. Classic immutable history + a movable current pointer.
3. Why version dataset items this way rather than simply updating in place?
  1. to speed up queries
  2. for reproducibility: a run pins a validFrom and always sees 'that version of the question'; otherwise historical scores lose their context and two experiments can't be fairly compared (Lesson 30's eval replays exactly via datasetItemValidFrom)
  3. to save storage
  4. to support concurrent writes
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Evaluation's value is comparison. If a question is edited in place, 'last time'\'s score loses context—you don't know what it tested, can't fairly compare with 'this time'. Versioning pins a time interval on every version, letting each run pin the version it used (Lesson 30's eval replays via datasetItemValidFrom). Same 'record truth without erasing' belief as Lesson 37's prompt versions and git commits.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Datasets link 'production traces' and 'test cases' via sourceTraceId, and use versioning to keep experiments reproducible. Imagine maintaining a customer-service bot: how would you use these two mechanisms to build a 'continuous improvement' regression-test flow? Which production cases are worth promoting into a dataset? When should you version-up a question rather than create a new one?

Dataset runs & run items

The last lesson built the "exam paper" (the dataset). This lesson is about how to "sit an exam"—a dataset run takes the whole set and quizzes the app end to end. Two focuses: each question, run once, produces a real trace, and a dataset run item is the record that pins "which exam × which question → which answer sheet (trace)" together; and these run items are mirrored to ClickHouse, snapshotting the question text along with them, so that "aggregate all questions' scores for this exam" is both fast and reproducible. Once you grasp runs and run items, you connect to Lesson 30's dataset-run-item-upsert eval trigger—run an experiment and the scores get computed automatically.

📋 Analogy
A dataset is the exam paper; a run is one sitting of the exam: with a specific model / prompt config, answer every question on the paper. Each answered question leaves a complete answer sheet (a trace recording the full input/output and every step). A run item is like an "answer-card stub": it doesn't store the answer itself, only a three-way correspondence—"this is exam #3, question #7, matching answer sheet #920". Once the graders (Lessons 29–32's scoring) score each sheet, aggregating all questions' scores for the same exam yields the exam's overall grade (this run averaged 0.82). So "swap a model, sit another exam—did the grade rise or fall" is obvious—exactly the basis for the next lesson's "experiment comparison".

The three-way link: which exam × which question → which answer sheet

DatasetRuns is simple: a sitting has a name, description, metadata, belongs to a dataset; run names are unique within a dataset ([datasetId, projectId, name]). The real key is DatasetRunItems: it links datasetRunId + datasetItemId to a traceId (plus an optional observationId). In other words, every (run × item) pair maps to one real trace—the run item is the carrier of this three-way relation.

run item = carrier of the three-way link: schema.prisma:719 DatasetRuns (unique [datasetId, projectId, name]), :739 DatasetRunItems links datasetRunId+datasetItemId to traceId(+observationId). A run executes the whole set once, each producing a real trace; the run item records "which exam × which question → which trace".
an examDatasetRuns
A run means "with a fixed config (model/prompt/version), run the whole dataset once". It has a name (e.g. "gpt-4o-2025-baseline"), description, metadata, belongs to a dataset. Run names are unique per dataset, so you can trace each experiment by name.
answer-card stubDatasetRunItems
The connector: datasetRunId (which exam) + datasetItemId (which question) → traceId (which sheet). It stores no input/output—those are in the trace it points to. It only binds "experiment / question / execution record" together, letting you follow a run down to each question's full execution detail.
reuse allthe trace is an ordinary trace
The beauty: a run's traces are indistinguishable from production traces—same observation tree (Lessons 13, 25), same ability to attach scores (Lesson 28), same ingestion path (Lesson 12). Experiments aren't a separate system but "a batch of ordinary traces fed by a dataset", reusing all of the first five parts.

Mirror to ClickHouse: snapshotting the question text too

A run item lives in Postgres (the relational truth), but to compute "what's this exam's average score, up or down from last time" you must aggregate over masses of run items × masses of scores—an OLAP job, belonging to ClickHouse (Lessons 17, 22). So run items are mirrored to CH's dataset_run_items_rmt table. The intriguing part: this table stores not just ids but denormalizes the run fields and snapshots the question text into it.

denormalize = trade space for aggregation speed + memory: the CH table inlines immutable run fields and snapshots item input/expectedOutput (the source comment: "denormalized … but snapshots are relevant"). Even if the question is later versioned up, this run still remembers the question text then—Lesson 34's reproducibility, doubly insured. Engine ReplacingMergeTree (same as Lesson 17). Source: clickhouse/migrations/clustered/0024_dataset_run_items.up.sql.
packages/shared/clickhouse/migrations/clustered/0024_dataset_run_items.up.sqlCH mirror table
CREATE TABLE dataset_run_items_rmt (
  id String, project_id String, dataset_run_id String,
  dataset_item_id String, dataset_id String, trace_id String, observation_id Nullable(String),
  -- denormalized: immutable run fields inlined (saves a JOIN)
  dataset_run_name String, dataset_run_description Nullable(String), dataset_run_created_at DateTime64(3),
  -- snapshot: the question text "as of then" (remembers what was tested even after a version bump)
  dataset_item_input Nullable(String) CODEC(ZSTD(3)),
  dataset_item_expected_output Nullable(String) CODEC(ZSTD(3)),
  event_ts DateTime64(3), is_deleted UInt8
) ENGINE = ReplicatedReplacingMergeTree(event_ts, is_deleted)   -- many writes per id, keep latest (L17)
ORDER BY (project_id, dataset_id, dataset_run_id, id);   -- sort key fits "aggregate by run"
dimensionPostgres (DatasetRunItem)ClickHouse (dataset_run_items_rmt)
rolerelational truth, transactionalaggregation copy, built for OLAP
storesthin: runId+itemId+traceId relationsfat: also inlines run name/desc + question snapshot
enginerow store, strongly consistentReplacingMergeTree(event_ts, is_deleted)
good atexact single-row reads, FK constraints"group by run, average" in one scan

This is Lesson 22's "dual-store" pattern replayed in the experiment domain: Postgres as the authority, ClickHouse as the snapshot. The two don't compete but divide labor—one keeps it correct, one keeps it fast.

Run an experiment, scores compute themselves: run-level aggregation

A run item points to a trace, the trace carries scores (Lesson 28). Walk that chain and you can compute each run's average score—exactly what dataset-run-items.ts does: JOIN the scores table, average by score name (agg_scores_avg). And where do the scores come from? Recall Lesson 30: creating a run item emits a dataset-run-item-upsert event, automatically triggering evaluators to score this run's traces. So run an experiment and the scores compute themselves, no manual work.

1

create run + execute per question

Create a run (createOrFetchDatasetRun), run the app once per item with the target config, producing a trace.

2

create run item (pin the version)

Record run×item→traceId, pinning the item's validFrom (L34). Mirror into CH, snapshot the question.

3

auto-trigger evaluation

Persisting the run item emits a dataset-run-item-upsert event → Lesson 30's createEvalJobs queues eval for this trace.

4

scores flow back

Judge / code / human (Lessons 29–32) produce scores, attached back to this trace via the ingestion path.

5

aggregate by run

dataset-run-items.ts JOINs scores, averages by name (agg_scores_avg), yielding "this run's average"—handed to Lesson 36 for comparison.

packages/shared/src/server/repositories/dataset-run-items.tsaggregate scores by run
// each run's average: JOIN scores, average by score name → agg_scores_avg
type DatasetRunRecord = {
  agg_scores_avg: Array<[string, number]>;   // e.g. [["helpfulness", 0.82], ["toxicity", 0.03]]
  scores: ScoreAggregate; …
};
// scoresCte: aggregate numeric scores as (name, avg_value)
WITH scores_aggregated AS ( SELECT …, avg(value) AS scores_avg … GROUP BY name )
// so "gpt-4o sitting vs claude sitting" compares directly on agg_scores_avg — next lesson's comparison

With that, Part 6's first two lessons assemble the full experiment skeleton: dataset (exam paper, L34) → run (a sitting) → run item (answer-card stub, pinning question to sheet) → trace (the sheet) → auto-scoring (Lessons 29–32) → aggregate by run (the exam's grade). The next lesson is the last step: place multiple runs' grades side by side and pick the best config—that's an "experiment".

🎯 Design trade-off
Why build a dedicated run-item table rather than just tag a trace with "which experiment it belongs to"? Because it's a many-to-many relation that must support efficient aggregation. A trace conceptually belongs to "some question of some run", but "run", "item", and "trace" each evolve independently (questions get versioned, runs get re-run, traces have full lifecycles). A dedicated junction table (the run item) carrying this three-way relation both keeps the three decoupled and independent, and gives you a clean aggregation entry: to compute "which questions this exam covered, what the average is", just scan this table and JOIN scores—rather than fishing for tagged needles in a vast trace ocean. Modeling the relation independently is the precondition for efficient query and aggregation.

Why does the CH mirror table denormalize and "snapshot" the run fields and question text? Two compounding reasons. One is aggregation speed (Lessons 17, 22's old theme): inlining run name and question text saves repeated back-to-table JOINs when computing "average grouped by run"—OLAP thrives on this. The other is subtler, memory: questions get versioned (Lesson 34), but a March experiment ought to forever remember the question text it tested then. Snapshotting input/expectedOutput into the run item at run time doubly insures "reproducibility"—even if the original question later changes beyond recognition, this experiment's context is preserved intact. The snapshot isn't redundancy but freezing "the truth as of then".
🎯 Key points
  • A run = an exam sitting: DatasetRuns runs the whole dataset with a fixed config; run names are unique per dataset, so each experiment is traceable by name.
  • A run item = carrier of the three-way link: DatasetRunItems pins datasetRunId+datasetItemId→traceId, storing no input/output—the answer is in the trace it points to. This keeps run/item/trace decoupled yet aggregatable.
  • A run's trace is an ordinary trace: same observation tree, same scores, same ingestion path—experiments reuse all of the first five parts, not a separate system.
  • Mirror to CH with a denormalized snapshot: dataset_run_items_rmt (ReplacingMergeTree) inlines run fields + snapshots question input/expectedOutput—both speeding "aggregate by run" and making an experiment forever remember what it tested (reproducibility, insured).
  • Run an experiment, scores auto-compute: creating a run item emits dataset-run-item-upsert → Lesson 30 auto-queues eval → scores flow back → dataset-run-items.ts JOINs scores, averages by name (agg_scores_avg) into "this run's grade", handed to Lesson 36 for comparison.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Langfuse's DatasetRunItem links datasetRunId + datasetItemId to a traceId. Why a dedicated junction table rather than just tagging a trace with 'which experiment it belongs to'?
  1. to encrypt experiment results
  2. to isolate experiment data from production data
  3. because traces can't take extra fields
  4. because it's a many-to-many relation needing efficient aggregation: run/item/trace evolve independently, a dedicated junction table decouples them and gives a clean aggregation entry (scan it and JOIN scores to compute 'this run's average', not fish tagged needles in a trace ocean)
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. A trace conceptually belongs to 'some question of some run', but run, item, and trace evolve independently (questions versioned, runs re-run, traces have full lifecycles). A junction table carrying the three-way relation decouples them and gives an aggregation entry: computing 'which questions this exam covered, what the average' just scans it and JOINs scores. Modeling the relation independently is the precondition for efficient query/aggregation.
2. In ClickHouse's dataset_run_items_rmt mirror table, besides the id/run/item/trace relations it denormalizes the run name and 'snapshots' the item's input/expectedOutput. What unique problem does snapshotting the question text solve?
  1. preventing question deletion
  2. memory: questions get versioned (L34), but the snapshot lets a March experiment forever remember the question text it tested then—even if the original later changes beyond recognition, the experiment's context is preserved, doubly insuring reproducibility
  3. encrypting the question
  4. compressing storage
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Denormalization has two payoffs. Aggregation speed: inlining run name/question saves back-to-table JOINs (the OLAP theme). Subtler is memory: questions get versioned, but a March experiment should forever remember March's question text. Snapshotting input/expectedOutput into the run item at run time preserves the experiment's context even if the original later changes—doubly insuring L34's reproducibility. The snapshot isn't redundancy but freezing 'the truth as of then'.
3. In Langfuse, 'running a dataset run computes scores automatically'. How is this auto-scoring triggered?
  1. creating a run item emits a dataset-run-item-upsert event, triggering Lesson 30's createEvalJobs to queue eval for this run's trace; once scores flow back, dataset-run-items.ts JOINs scores and averages by name (agg_scores_avg)
  2. ClickHouse computes it automatically
  3. the frontend polls and scores every second
  4. manually clicking score when the run finishes
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. Recall Lesson 30: createEvalJobs has three trigger sources, one being dataset-run-item-upsert. Creating a run item emits this event → evaluators filter-match this run's trace → queue a JobExecution → judge/code/human produce scores attached back via ingestion. Finally dataset-run-items.ts JOINs scores and averages by name into agg_scores_avg (the run's grade), handed to Lesson 36. The whole chain is automatic.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The traces a dataset run produces are 'indistinguishable' from production traces—same observation tree, same scores, same ingestion path. What are the benefits of this 'experiments reuse production infrastructure' design? Imagine if experiment data went through a completely separate storage and query stack—what duplication and inconsistency risks would that bring?

Experiments & comparison

The last two lessons built the "exam paper" (the dataset) and the "exam mechanism" (the run). This lesson is about using them to make decisions—that's an experiment. One experiment takes a specific prompt + model config, runs it automatically over the whole dataset (producing one run), then places multiple runs side by side to pick the best. It turns "is changing this prompt or model actually better" from gut feeling into data—the closest thing to the "scientific method" in LLM engineering. Three focuses: how an experiment runs server-side automatically (fill the question into the prompt's variables, call the LLM, produce a trace); how scoring auto-connects (reusing Lessons 30 and 35); and how comparison uses baseline + deltas so you see gains/losses at a glance. This lesson also sets up Part 7's prompt management—the prompt being tested in an experiment is the next lesson's protagonist.

📋 Analogy
An experiment is like a rigorous A/B controlled trial. The same questions (dataset): group A uses "gpt-4o + prompt v1", group B uses "claude + prompt v2", each sits the exam properly. You needn't proctor yourself: the system automatically sets the questions (fills each question's input into the prompt's variable blanks), automatically grades (Lessons 29–32's scoring), automatically computes the grade (Lesson 35's aggregate-by-run). Finally it lays the report cards side by side, and lets you anoint one group as the baseline, so the rest show "how much above/below baseline". So the question that vexes everyone—"is my prompt change actually better or worse?"—no longer rests on feeling, but on a side-by-side report card with deltas.

Run server-side automatically: fill the question into prompt variables, call the LLM per item

Langfuse experiments can run entirely server-side: you just pick a dataset, a prompt with variables, and a model—the worker does the rest. createExperimentJobClickhouse (driven by the ExperimentCreate queue) does three steps per question: fill the question's input into the prompt's variable blanks, call the LLM with your chosen provider/model, and write the result as a trace + run item.

experiment = run a prompt server-side per question: processLLMCall (delegated by processItem) calls replaceVariablesInPrompt(prompt, item.input, …) to fill variables, calls the LLM with config.provider/model, writes a trace (environment=PromptExperiments, linking prompt, metadata with itemVersion=item.validFrom). Source: worker/src/features/experiments/experimentServiceClickhouse.ts:154-225,287.
worker/src/features/experiments/experimentServiceClickhouse.tsprocessLLMCall core (processItem delegates)
// 1) fill this question's input into the prompt's variable blanks → full messages
messages = replaceVariablesInPrompt(
  config.validatedPrompt, datasetItem.input, config.allVariables, config.placeholderNames);

// 2) build the trace: tag experiment env, link the prompt under test, pin item version
traceSinkParams = {
  environment: LangfuseInternalTraceEnvironment.PromptExperiments,
  traceId, prompt: config.prompt,
  experimentContext: { …, itemVersion: convert...(datasetItem.validFrom), itemExpectedOutput } };

// 3) call the LLM with the provider/model under test (result becomes this trace + run item)
modelParams = { provider: config.provider, model: config.model,
  adapter: config.validatedApiKey.adapter, ...config.model_params };
dimensionserver-side experiment (this lesson)remote / SDK experiment
who runs the appthe Langfuse worker (your chosen prompt/model)your own code / external system
how triggeredclick "run" in the UI → ExperimentCreate queuethe dataset's remoteExperimentUrl calls back your service
suitspure prompt/model comparison, zero-codecomplex custom pipelines (multi-step agents, retrieval, etc.)
in commonboth produce run + run item + trace, both use the same auto-scoring and comparison—downstream is identical

Both modes converge: whoever runs the app, the result lands as Lesson 35's run item + trace, enjoying the same auto-scoring (Lesson 30) and comparison views. "How the answer sheet is produced" can differ; "how it's judged and compared" is fully unified—another "one downstream, many upstreams" decoupling.

Comparison: anoint one as baseline, view the rest as deltas

Once the experiment runs, each trace is auto-scored and aggregated by run via Lessons 30 and 35, so every run has a "report card" (the average of each score name). The essence of comparison isn't absolute scores but side-by-side + deltas: you anoint one run as the baseline, and the rest show "how much above/below baseline". Langfuse's experiment comparison page (ExperimentComparisonSelector / ExperimentBaselineControls / ExperimentChartsGrid) does exactly this.

Comparison via "baseline + deltas": anoint one run as baseline, each score shows its relative delta (up/down). Source: web/src/features/experiments/components/'s ExperimentBaselineControls, ExperimentComparisonSelector, ExperimentChartsGrid.

pick a baseline

ExperimentBaselineControls lets you anoint one run as the reference—usually the "production-active" config, so all candidates compare to "the status quo", answering "would changing be better".

choose runs to compare

ExperimentComparisonSelector picks several runs side by side. Same questions, different configs, fairly comparable (Lesson 34's versioning guarantees they tested the same questions).

view multi-dimensional deltas

ExperimentChartsGrid charts each score dimension as a comparison. Goodness is often a multi-dimensional trade-off (more accurate but slower, costlier); the delta view makes the trade-off obvious.

The loop: a scientific "verify before you change" method

String Part 6's three lessons together and you get a continuous-improvement flywheel. It turns "optimizing an LLM app"—usually mystical—into a repeatable, data-backed engineering process:

1

build a dataset

Promote real botched production cases into test questions (L34), keeping the exam close to the real distribution.

2

run an experiment

For a candidate prompt/model config, the server runs a run over the whole set (this lesson), producing a batch of traces.

3

auto-score

Lessons 30, 35 automatically score each trace and aggregate this run's report card.

4

compare & decide

Side by side with the baseline (active config), view deltas: truly better → ship, regressed → reject—not by feeling.

5

back to step 1

After shipping you hit new hard cases, promote them into questions… the flywheel turns, the app grows steadier.

That's the whole value of Part 6: it upgrades Part 5's "can score quality" into "can make decisions with scores". And the core variable the experiment keeps testing—the prompt—is the protagonist of the next part (Part 7): how it's versioned, served at speed, and tuned interactively in the Playground. Evaluation tells you "how good now", experiments tell you "would changing be better", and prompt management gives you the confidence to change safely.

🎯 Design trade-off
Why does Langfuse run experiments server-side for you rather than just letting you run locally and upload results? Both are supported (the dataset's remoteExperimentUrl is for local/external runs), but server-side has unique value: zero-code, strongly consistent. A product manager or non-engineer can pick a dataset, tweak a prompt, and click "run" in the UI—no script needed; and server-side runs unify variable substitution, version pinning, eval scheduling, and environment tagging—baking "how to do an experiment right" into the platform, rather than hoping every person's local script is rigorous. The cost is the platform bearing LLM-call cost and concurrency, so it goes through the ExperimentCreate queue, reusing Lesson 30's scheduling resilience.

Why compare with "baseline + deltas" rather than absolute scores? Because absolute scores mean almost nothing; relative change is what matters. Is "helpfulness 0.78" good or bad? Without a reference you can't say. But "0.07 above the active config" is a fact you can act on. More importantly, quality is often a multi-dimensional trade-off: config B is more accurate but slower and costlier. Laying each dimension's delta side by side lets a decision-maker make the clear-eyed trade-off "is this much accuracy gain worth this much latency cost"—rather than being led by a single metric. This echoes Lesson 33's monitor "alert only on change": what carries information is always "the change", not "the absolute value".
🎯 Key points
  • An experiment = prompt × dataset × model, run server-side: createExperimentJobClickhouse per question uses replaceVariablesInPrompt to fill variables, calls the chosen provider/model, produces a trace + run item—all zero-code.
  • Experiment traces carry rich context: environment tagged PromptExperiments, linking the prompt under test (L37), metadata pinning itemVersion=validFrom (L34/35 version replay)—reproducible and traceable.
  • Scoring auto-connects: reusing Lesson 30's dataset-run-item-upsert trigger and Lesson 35's aggregate-by-run, a report card appears once the run finishes, no manual work.
  • Comparison via baseline + deltas: ExperimentBaselineControls picks the baseline, ExperimentComparisonSelector chooses runs, ExperimentChartsGrid charts multi-dimensional deltas—absolute scores are hard to judge, relative change drives decisions.
  • Part 6 closes the loop: dataset → experiment → score → compare → decide → (new hard cases flow back to the dataset). It upgrades "can score" into "can decide with scores"; the next part's prompt gives you the confidence to "change safely".

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. What does Langfuse's server-side experiment (createExperimentJobClickhouse) do for each dataset question?
  1. scores the item directly without calling an LLM
  2. runs the model in the frontend via JavaScript
  3. replaceVariablesInPrompt fills this question's input into the prompt's variable blanks, calls the LLM once with the chosen provider/model, writing a trace (tagged PromptExperiments env, linking the prompt under test, pinning the item version) + run item
  4. just copies the item to a new table
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: C. processLLMCall's three steps: ① replaceVariablesInPrompt fills the prompt's variables with item.input into full messages; ② build the trace, tagging env PromptExperiments, linking config.prompt, metadata pinning itemVersion=validFrom (L34/35 version replay); ③ call the LLM with config.provider/model. This is 'zero-code server-side experiments'—a PM can click run in the UI, with the platform unifying variable substitution/version pinning/eval scheduling.
2. The experiment comparison page shows results via 'baseline + deltas' rather than listing absolute scores. Why does this aid decisions?
  1. absolute scores mean almost nothing ('helpfulness 0.78' good or bad?), relative change does ('0.07 above active' is actionable); and quality is often a multi-dimensional trade-off, with side-by-side deltas making 'is the accuracy gain worth the latency cost' obvious
  2. absolute scores leak privacy
  3. a baseline encrypts results
  4. deltas use less CPU
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. 'Helpfulness 0.78' can't be judged without a reference, but '0.07 above the active config' is an actionable fact. More importantly quality is often a multi-dimensional trade-off (B more accurate but slower/costlier); laying each dimension's delta side by side lets decision-makers trade off clearly rather than be led by one metric. This echoes Lesson 33's 'alert only on change'—information lives in change, not absolutes. ExperimentBaselineControls/ComparisonSelector/ChartsGrid implement this.
3. Stringing Part 6's three lessons together, the 'dataset→experiment→score→compare→decide' loop essentially upgrades Part 5's capability into what?
  1. into real-time monitoring
  2. into a faster database
  3. into auto-writing prompts
  4. from 'can score quality' into 'can make decisions with scores': verify on a fixed test set before changing a prompt/model, judging by data not feeling—the closest thing to the scientific method in LLM engineering
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. Part 5 lets you 'see and score'; Part 6 lets you 'decide on that basis'. The dataset is a fixed exam, an experiment is a controlled trial, comparison is a report card with deltas—so 'is this change good' goes from gut feeling to data. The loop self-reinforces: post-ship hard cases get promoted into questions, the flywheel steadies the app. The repeatedly-tested prompt is the next part's protagonist.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Experiments turn 'verify before changing a prompt' into a scientific method. But it rests on premises: your dataset represents real traffic, and your scoring reflects real quality. Imagine one premise fails (e.g. a narrow dataset, or an LLM judge with systematic bias)—how would the comparison's conclusions mislead you? How would you guard against this 'the evaluation system itself is unreliable' risk? (Hint: revisit Lesson 32's human annotation as ground truth.)

Prompt management

The previous part kept "examining" one thing—the prompt. It's the "script" of your conversation with the model, the most frequently changed part of an LLM app: rewording one word, adding or removing one instruction, can swing the outcome wildly. This lesson is about how Langfuse manages prompts like git manages code: immutable versions, movable labels, a commit message per change, plus dependency composition that lets one prompt reference another. Grasp this "version control" model and you hold the key to "changing prompts safely"—you can release, roll back instantly, and reproduce any historical version exactly, all without touching a line of application code.

📋 Analogy
Prompt management is almost git tailored for prompts. Each time you edit a prompt and save, it creates a new version (like a commit—an auto-incrementing number, content never changed again), with an optional change note (commitMessage). And labels like "production" / "staging" are like git's branches / tags—they're movable pointers, each pointing to one version. Point production at v5 and you've "released" v5; if something's wrong, point it back at v4 for an "instant rollback"—and your application code doesn't change one character, because it asks for "the production one", not a specific version number. You can even have one prompt reference another (a dependency), like import-ing a shared module in code—write the shared preamble once, reuse it everywhere.

Immutable versions + movable labels

A prompt is uniquely identified by (name, version) (@@unique([projectId, name, version])). On creating a version, version = previous + 1—the number auto-increments, and once written the content is never changed again. labels is a string array, with the key rule: a given label can point to only one version under a name. So when you tag a new version with production, the system automatically strips that label from the old version—the label thus "moves" to the new one.

git-style version control: version auto-increments and is immutable (latestPrompt.version + 1); a label is unique and movable—tagging a new version with production auto-strips it from the old one (removeLabelsFromPreviousPromptVersions). A new version always gets the latest label. Source: createPrompt.ts:88-181, constants.ts:1-2 (PRODUCTION_LABEL/LATEST_PROMPT_LABEL).
web/src/features/prompts/server/actions/createPrompt.tsversion increment + label move
// find the current latest version; new = it + 1 (first is 1)—number auto-increments, content immutable
const latestPrompt = await prisma.prompt.findFirst({ where: {…name}, orderBy: [{ version: "desc" }] });
version: latestPrompt?.version ? latestPrompt.version + 1 : 1,

// a newly created prompt always gets the 'latest' label
const finalLabels = [...labels, LATEST_PROMPT_LABEL];   // "latest"

// key: a label is unique per name — labels put on the new version must be stripped from old ones
await removeLabelsFromPreviousPromptVersions({ …, labelsToRemove: finalLabels });
// so the production pointer "moves" to the new version; old versions no longer carry it
git conceptprompt management counterpartnote
commitversionimmutable snapshot, auto-incrementing number, content never changed
commit messagecommitMessagea note per change, leaving a traceable trail
branch / taglabels (production/latest…)movable pointers, unique per name, pointing to a version
repo-level labelstagsconsistent across all versions (on the name, not a version)
import a modulePromptDependencyone prompt references another, composition reuse

Keep this mapping in mind and prompt management is no longer a new concept—it's the git you already know, just managing not code but "the script of your conversation with the model".

Fetch by label: fully divorce "releasing" from "writing code"

This git-style model's biggest practical value hides in how the app fetches a prompt. Production code shouldn't hardcode "give me v5"—it should say "give me the production one". So the decision of "which version to use" is lifted out of code deployment into a label-moving operations action. Releases and rollbacks thus become second-scale, and bypass any code change, CI, or deploy flow.

fetch by label (production-preferred)

get("greeting", label="production"): the app only ever knows the production pointer, using whatever it points to. Switching versions is operations moving a label in the UI, with the app oblivious. A textbook of "config-code separation".

fetch by version (pinned)

get("greeting", version=5): locks exactly to version 5, never drifting. Suits absolute-reproducibility scenarios (a formal experiment, reproducing a paper).

protected labels (against fat-fingering)

PromptProtectedLabels lets you mark key labels like production as "protected"—moving it needs higher permission, preventing a newcomer from accidentally shipping an untested version.

config-code separation: the app fetches by label="production", with which version the pointer targets decided by operations in the UI. Release = move the label to v5; rollback = move it back to v4—second-scale, zero-code, zero-deploy. Key labels can be gated via PromptProtectedLabels.

Dependencies: let one prompt reference another

With many prompts comes duplication—the same "You are a friendly support assistant…" preamble might appear in ten prompts. Editing it once means editing ten places, easy to miss. PromptDependency solves this: a prompt (parent) can reference another prompt (child), either pinned by version (childVersion) or floating by label (childLabel). It's like import-ing a shared module in code—write the shared snippet once, reuse it everywhere.

composition is reuse: schema.prisma:864's PromptDependency uses parentId + childName + childLabel (floating) or childVersion (pinned). Floating makes a shared snippet update everywhere on one edit; pinning shields a parent from a child upgrade—mirroring the first section's label/version trade-off.
packages/shared/prisma/schema.prisma:864 · createPrompt.tsdependency: floating or pinned
model PromptDependency {
  parentId  String   // the referrer (parent prompt)
  childName String   // the referenced prompt's name
  childLabel   String?  // one of: float by label (e.g. "production"), parent follows child upgrades
  childVersion Int?     // or: pin by version (e.g. 3), parent always uses this, no drift
}
// on create, dep.type decides which to pin (createPrompt.ts)
...(dep.type === "version" ? { childVersion: dep.version } : { childLabel: dep.label })

String this mechanism together and "changing a prompt safely" becomes a clear pipeline—dovetailing with the next lessons' Playground (L39) and all of Part 6's experiments:

1

edit → save as a new version

Saving an edit creates a new version (auto-incrementing, with a commitMessage); the old version is preserved intact.

2

try in the Playground

Try the new version on a few cases in the Playground (L39), or pull it into a dataset to run an experiment (L36) for a quantitative comparison.

3

satisfied → move the production label

Confirmed better, move the production pointer to the new version—the production app's next prompt fetch picks it up, zero-deploy.

4

trouble → instant rollback

If production misbehaves, move production back to the old version—the old one is always there, rollback loses no data and waits for no release.

🎯 Design trade-off
Why are versions immutable while labels are movable? This "immutable entity + movable pointer" is the essence of version control—git is so, Lesson 34's dataset items are so, prompts here too. Immutability guarantees "reproducibility": v5 is forever v5, and the version you ran in production three months ago can be pulled up today, word for word, for a post-mortem. Movable labels provide "evolvability": reality needs a stable name (production) to denote "the current active one", and the active one changes. Together, you can both iterate boldly (every edit safely preserved) and operate calmly (switch smoothly via labels, roll back anytime). Cleanly assigning the immutable/mutable tension onto the two concepts "version" and "label" is the elegance of data modeling.

Why is "fetch by label" the key to production decoupling? Because it fully moves the decision "which prompt version to use" out of the code. If the app hardcodes version=5, switching prompts means changing code, passing CI, redeploying—slow, heavy, and needs an engineer present. With label="production", switching becomes operations clicking a pointer-move in the UI: second-scale, rollback-able, doable by non-engineers. This is the power of "config-code separation"—lifting the frequently-changing thing (prompt content) out of the slowly-changing thing (app code), each evolving at its own pace. PromptProtectedLabels then gates key labels, keeping "second-scale releases" flexible without losing control.
🎯 Key points
  • A prompt = git tailored for prompts: (name, version) unique; the version number auto-increments, content immutable, with a commitMessage—every edit safely preserved and exactly reproducible.
  • A label is a movable pointer: production/latest labels are unique per name, pointing to one version; tagging a new version auto-strips the label from the old one (removeLabelsFromPreviousPromptVersions)—moving a label = release / rollback.
  • Fetch by label = config-code separation: production does get(label="production"), switching versions just needs operations moving a pointer—second-scale, zero-code, zero-deploy, rollback-able; fetch by version is for absolute reproducibility.
  • Protected labels against fat-fingering: PromptProtectedLabels gates key labels like production with permissions, preventing an untested version from being shipped by accident.
  • Dependencies = prompt composition reuse: PromptDependency lets a parent reference a child by childLabel (floating) or childVersion (pinned)—write a shared snippet once, reuse everywhere, with trade-off logic mirroring label/version.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Langfuse designs a prompt's version as immutable (auto-incrementing, content never changed) and a label (like production) as a movable pointer. The core benefit of this 'immutable entity + movable pointer' combo is?
  1. encrypting prompt content
  2. auto-optimizing prompts
  3. saving database space
  4. getting both 'reproducibility' and 'evolvability': immutable versions let any historical version be replayed exactly, movable labels let a stable name like production switch/roll back smoothly—cleanly assigning the tension to two concepts
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: D. This is the essence of version control—git, Lesson 34's items, prompts alike. Immutability guarantees reproducibility: v5 is forever v5, the version in production three months ago is replayable word-for-word today. Movable labels provide evolvability: reality needs a stable name like production for 'the current active', which changes. Together you iterate boldly (each version preserved) and operate calmly (smooth switch/instant rollback via labels).
2. Langfuse recommends production code fetch a prompt via get(label="production") rather than get(version=5). The biggest operational value of this is?
  1. fetching by label is faster
  2. lifting 'which version' out of code: switching versions becomes operations moving a pointer in the UI—second-scale, rollback-able, doable by non-engineers, no code change/CI/redeploy (config-code separation)
  3. fetching by label saves tokens
  4. fetching by version can't get old versions
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. If the app hardcodes version=5, switching means changing code, passing CI, redeploying—slow, heavy, needs an engineer. With label="production", switching is operations clicking a pointer-move in the UI: second-scale, rollback-able, non-engineer-doable. This lifts the frequently-changing thing (prompt content) out of the slowly-changing thing (app code), each evolving at its own pace. Protected labels then gate key labels.
3. PromptDependency lets a parent prompt reference a child, either floating by childLabel (e.g. production) or pinned by childVersion (e.g. 3). What does each reference style suit?
  1. pinning auto-upgrades
  2. floating (label): the parent auto-gets updates when the child upgrades, suiting 'edit the shared snippet once, effective everywhere'; pinned (version): the parent always uses that version, unaffected by child upgrades, suiting stability—the same trade-off as fetching prompts by label/version
  3. the two are equivalent
  4. floating is safer, pinning is risky
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. PromptDependency describes a reference by either childLabel or childVersion. Floating (childLabel): follows the label, the parent auto-gets new content on child upgrade—suiting a shared preamble's 'edit once, update everywhere'. Pinned (childVersion): locks a specific version, the parent unaffected by upgrades—suiting absolute stability/reproducibility. This mirrors the first section's 'fetch by label vs version': float for convenience, pin for stability.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • Prompt management ports the whole idea of 'software version control' (immutable versions, movable labels, dependencies, commit messages) onto prompts. Recall your git experience: which git workflows (branch strategy, code review, rollback drills) do you think are worth porting to prompt management? Conversely, how do prompts differ fundamentally from code, making some git practices not necessarily applicable?

Prompt serving & caching

The last lesson let you manage prompts safely. But production has a real problem: an app may fetch a prompt before every single LLM call ("give me the production one"). Hitting Postgres each time is slow and crushes the database. This lesson is about how PromptService uses Redis to make prompt serving fast and steady, focusing on two mechanisms: the classic read-through cache, and an exceptionally elegant "swap the namespace, don't delete keys one by one" epoch invalidation strategy. Along the way we'll see Lesson 37's dependencies' "second half": referenced child prompts are resolved and inlined into one complete prompt at serving time—and it's precisely this fact, "one prompt may span multiple names", that forces the clever invalidation design.

📋 Analogy
Prompt serving is like a library's popular-books fast-access shelf. A reader (the app) comes to borrow a book (prompt); the librarian first checks the fast shelf (Redis): if it's there, hand it over instantly; if not, run to the main stacks (Postgres) to fetch one and drop a copy on the fast shelf, so the next person is quick. Then the hard part: a book gets a new edition—how do you stop everyone from still borrowing the old one? Combing the whole shelf to pull old editions one by one is too hard—besides, one book may have chapters of other books tucked inside (Lesson 37's dependencies), so one change ripples everywhere. The library's trick: hang a new "edition tag" on the entire fast shelf. Books under the old tag are no longer recognized (the rule only honors the current tag); they needn't be cleaned, just left to expire; and every new borrow goes through the shelf with the new tag. Swapping a tag is far faster and steadier than pulling books one by one.

The read-through cache: ask Redis first, fall back to the DB

The core method is PromptService.getPrompt, a textbook read-through cache: check Redis first, return on a hit; only on a miss go back to Postgres (findPrompt), and after fetching, write it into Redis (with a TTL), so the next identical request hits the cache. Whether the cache is on is controlled by LANGFUSE_CACHE_PROMPT_ENABLED, and how long each entry lives by LANGFUSE_CACHE_PROMPT_TTL_SECONDS.

read-through + backfill: getPrompt first getCachedPrompt, returns on a hit; on a miss runs findPrompt (DB) then cachePrompt (redis.set(key, value, "EX", ttlSeconds)). Toggle LANGFUSE_CACHE_PROMPT_ENABLED, duration LANGFUSE_CACHE_PROMPT_TTL_SECONDS. Source: PromptService/index.ts:49-86,166-173.
packages/shared/src/server/services/PromptService/index.tsgetPrompt read-through
public async getPrompt(params): Promise<PromptResult | null> {
  if (this.cacheEnabled) {
    const cachedPrompt = await this.getCachedPrompt(params);   // ① ask Redis first
    if (cachedPrompt) return cachedPrompt;                     // hit → return instantly, no DB
  }
  const dbPrompt = await this.findPrompt(params);              // ② miss → query Postgres
  if (this.cacheEnabled && dbPrompt)
    await this.cachePrompt({ ...params, prompt: dbPrompt });    // ③ backfill Redis (with TTL)
  return dbPrompt;
}
// cachePrompt: redis.set(key, value, "EX", this.ttlSeconds)  — every entry self-expires
casewhich pathlatencytouches Postgres?
cache hit (vast majority)Redis returns directlymillisecondsno
cache miss (cold/just invalidated)query DB → backfill Redis → returnone DB round-tripyes (only this one)
TTL expirynext read becomes a miss, auto-backfillssame, single requestyes (single)

In a phrase: the database only bears "the first request", then Redis catches them all. That's the value of read-through—amortizing a "read on every call" high-frequency access into "one DB read per TTL period".

Resolution: inline dependencies into one complete prompt

Lesson 37 said prompts can reference other prompts. But what the app ultimately gets must be a complete, self-contained prompt—the runtime can't be left to assemble the referenced snippets on the fly. So serving includes a resolve step: buildAndResolvePromptGraph walks the dependency graph, finds each referenced child, and inlines it into the parent, producing an "expanded" finished product. Two safety gates are essential: a nesting depth cap (MAX_PROMPT_NESTING_DEPTH) and cycle detection (a seen set), preventing A-depends-on-B-depends-on-A from hanging the service.

resolution = expand composition into a product: buildAndResolvePromptGraph walks Lesson 37's dependency graph, inlining children into the parent to get resolvedPrompt; MAX_PROMPT_NESTING_DEPTH caps depth, a seen set blocks cycles. What's cached and returned is this expanded product. Source: PromptService/index.ts:132-144,218+.
packages/shared/src/server/services/PromptService/index.tsresolve + two gates
// resolve: return the product with dependencies inlined
public async resolvePrompt(prompt) {
  const promptGraph = await this.buildAndResolvePromptGraph(...);
  return { ...prompt, prompt: promptGraph.resolvedPrompt };   // children inlined
}
// recursively inline along the graph, two safety gates:
if (level >= MAX_PROMPT_NESTING_DEPTH) throw …;   // gate1: stop if nesting too deep
const seen = new Set<string>();                    // gate2: track visited, catch A→B→A instantly

why resolve at serving time

Dependencies are stored in the DB as "reference relations" (pointing to a child by label/version). But a child's label moves and its content changes—so only at the moment of fetching can you pull the current child and inline it, getting the product correct as of now.

gate 1: depth cap

MAX_PROMPT_NESTING_DEPTH limits dependency nesting levels. It prevents someone from making the chain so deep that one resolution pulls dozens of prompts and drags down the service.

gate 2: cycle detection

The seen set records visited prompts. The instant a cycle appears—A depends on B, B depends on A—it's caught and errors out; otherwise the recursion spins forever.

Epoch invalidation: swap the namespace, don't delete keys one by one

The hardest part of caching is always invalidation: when a prompt changes (new version, label move), the old cache must be voided. The difficulty—a resolved prompt may span several names (via dependencies), so you can't even say "which keys to delete". Langfuse's solution is elegant to the point of slyness: embed a project-scoped epoch token in the cache key; to invalidate, delete no keys, just rotate that epoch token to a new random value—so all old keys are instantly "orphaned" (new reads/writes use the new epoch in a new namespace), and the old ones simply expire by TTL.

O(1) invalidation, no scan-and-delete: invalidateCache just SETs a new random token (48 bits of entropy) on the project-scoped epoch key. Keys under the old epoch are no longer hit by any read and die off by their own TTL; the epoch key itself has a 7-day TTL. getOrCreateEpoch uses SET NX for concurrent initialization. Source: PromptService/index.ts:179-260.
packages/shared/src/server/services/PromptService/index.tsepoch invalidation + key
// invalidate: delete no keys, just rotate the project-scoped epoch token → all old keys orphaned
public async invalidateCache({ projectId }) {
  await this.redis.set(this.getEpochKey({ projectId }), this.newEpochToken(),
                       "EX", this.epochTtlSeconds);   // epoch key lives 7 days
}
// embed epoch in the key: change the epoch and the whole namespace turns the page
getCacheKey = `prompt:${projectId}:${epoch}:${promptName}:${version ?? label}`;
// epoch project-scoped (not prompt-scoped): a resolved prompt spans names, so any change invalidates all
getEpochKey = `prompt_cache_epoch:${projectId}`;

This design turns "invalidating a tangle of related caches" from an intractable precise-deletion problem into an O(1) token swap. The only cost is old keys briefly occupying a little memory until they expire—trading a touch of wasted memory for invalidation logic that is maximally simple and correct. It's a distributed-caching move well worth adding to your toolbox.

comparisondelete keys one by one (naive)rotate epoch (Langfuse)
invalidation complexityfind all affected keys, O(N) with a scanSET one new token, O(1)
dependency trackingmust reverse-lookup "which resolved products contain this child"none needed—the whole namespace turns the page
miss riskone miss serves a stale promptimpossible to miss, all old keys orphaned
costcomplex, error-prone logicold keys hold a little memory until expiry (auto-die)
🎯 Design trade-off
Why invalidate by "rotating the epoch" rather than dutifully deleting the old keys? Because precise deletion is nearly infeasible here. A served prompt is a resolved product that may inline several other prompts (Lesson 37 dependencies). When you change any one child, all resolved products that indirectly depend on it should be invalidated—but you can barely reverse-lookup "which keys are affected", let alone the fact that they're scattered across different names. "Find them one by one and delete" is slow and miss-prone, and one miss means someone reads a stale prompt. Rotating the epoch cuts the knot: don't search, don't delete, just swap to a fresh namespace; the old ones are all voided and expire naturally. Invalidation is now O(1) and never misses. The cost is a little wasted memory before old keys expire—a textbook "trade space for correctness and simplicity".

Why is the epoch "project-scoped" rather than "prompt-scoped"? A direct corollary of the above. If the epoch were isolated per prompt name, then on changing a child prompt you'd again have to figure out "which parents depend on it, and should I rotate their epochs too"—the dependency-tracking problem reborn. Putting the epoch at project granularity means "any prompt change in this project turns the page on the whole project's prompt cache". A bit coarse—occasionally invalidating caches that could have been kept—but it buys the certainty of never needing to track dependencies at all. Between "invalidate a little less" and "never read stale data", it firmly chooses the latter—exactly right for prompts, where correctness matters far more than cache hit rate.
🎯 Key points
  • Prompt serving must be fast: production may fetch a prompt on every LLM call; hitting Postgres directly is too slow and heavy, so PromptService puts a Redis cache in front.
  • Read-through cache: getPrompt checks Redis first, returns on a hit; on a miss falls back to Postgres then backfills Redis (SET EX ttl). Toggle LANGFUSE_CACHE_PROMPT_ENABLED, duration ..._TTL_SECONDS.
  • Resolve dependencies at serving time: buildAndResolvePromptGraph inlines referenced children into a self-contained product, with a depth cap + seen cycle check; what's cached/returned is this resolved product.
  • Epoch invalidation (the gem): the cache key embeds a project-scoped epoch token; to invalidate, delete no keys, just rotate the token to a new random value—all old keys orphaned, expiring by TTL. O(1), never misses, no scan.
  • Why epoch is project-scoped: a resolved prompt spans names, so precise deletion / prompt-scoped isolation would require dependency tracking; project-scoped epoch trades "a little over-invalidation" for "no tracking + never stale"—the right call for correctness-first data like prompts.

🧪 Self-test - think about the design

1. Langfuse's PromptService uses Redis as a read-through cache. What flow does 'read-through' specifically mean?
  1. queries Postgres every time, then updates Redis on the side
  2. getPrompt checks Redis first, returns on a hit (no DB); only on a miss queries Postgres and backfills the result into Redis (SET EX ttl), so the next identical request hits the cache—the vast majority are millisecond and don't load the DB
  3. writes Redis first, then async to the DB
  4. reads only Redis, never the DB
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. Read-through: a hit takes the shortcut, only a miss penetrates to the authoritative DB and backfills. getPrompt first getCachedPrompt, returns on hit; on miss findPrompt(DB)→cachePrompt(redis.set EX ttlSeconds). Production may fetch a prompt every LLM call; this cache shields the DB, with only the first request after a cold/invalidated cache hitting the DB. Toggle LANGFUSE_CACHE_PROMPT_ENABLED, duration _TTL_SECONDS.
2. After a prompt change the cache must be invalidated. Langfuse doesn't delete old keys one by one but embeds a project-scoped epoch token in the cache key, rotating it to a new random value on invalidation. Why?
  1. because a resolved prompt may inline several other prompts (dependencies), precisely reverse-looking-up 'which keys are affected' is nearly infeasible and one miss serves stale data; rotating the epoch swaps to a fresh namespace, old keys orphaned and expiring by TTL—O(1), never misses
  2. to save Redis memory
  3. because Redis doesn't support DEL
  4. to make the cache never expire
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: A. A served prompt is a resolved product that may inline several children (L37). Changing one child should invalidate all resolved products indirectly depending on it, but you can barely reverse-lookup the affected keys, scattered across names—deleting one by one is slow and miss-prone. invalidateCache just SETs a new token on the project-scoped epoch key; cache keys embed that epoch, so old keys are all orphaned and expire by TTL. Trade a little wasted memory for simple, correct invalidation.
3. Why is Langfuse's cache epoch 'project-scoped' (prompt_cache_epoch:{projectId}) rather than isolated per prompt name?
  1. prompt-scoped epochs aren't supported by Redis
  2. because a resolved prompt spans multiple names (dependencies); if the epoch were per prompt name, changing a child would again require tracking 'which parents depend on it'; a project-scoped epoch trades 'occasionally over-invalidating' for 'no dependency tracking at all + never stale'
  3. to share cache across projects
  4. project-scoped keys are shorter, saving memory
Show answer & explanation click to expand
Answer: B. The source comment states: the epoch is project-scoped because a resolved prompt can include transitive dependencies across multiple prompt names. Per-name isolation would revive the 'which parents are affected' dependency-tracking problem on a child change. Project granularity means 'any prompt change in the project turns the whole prompt cache's page'—coarser, occasionally over-invalidating, but buying certainty of no dependency tracking. Right for prompts, where correctness beats hit rate.
💭 Open questions (no single right answer - just think or try)
  • The epoch-invalidation move—'change the namespace, not delete keys'—is essentially 'trade space for correctness and simplicity'. Recall caching systems you've built or imagined: which invalidation scenarios gave you headaches (cascading invalidation, dependency tracking, thundering herd)? Could this epoch/version-namespace idea apply? How would you weigh its costs (memory, TTL choice, cold-start thundering herd)?

Playground & LLM connections

This lesson caps Part 7 and the whole "developer workflow" arc. The Playground is where you interactively test a prompt—type a few messages, pick a model, hit run, see the response, and once satisfied, go version it (Lesson 37) and run an experiment (Lesson 36). But for any server-side LLM call to happen, two things must exist behind the scenes: a safely stored LLM connection (your provider credentials), and a set of defined structured-output schemas and tools. This lesson covers them, focusing on an unavoidable security topic: a provider's API key is a "crown jewel" that can spend your real money, so how does Langfuse store it encrypted and safely retrieve it at the moment of use. You'll also see a guide-wide convergence: the Playground, evaluation (Lesson 29), and experiments (Lesson 36) share one LLM-call engine.

📋 Analogy
The Playground is like a "driving simulator + key safe". The simulator (playground) lets you test-drive (test a prompt) repeatedly before going on the road (versioning, shipping)—and it uses the real car's same engine (the same fetchLLMCompletion as evals and experiments), so what runs smoothly in the sim runs smoothly on the road. But starting the engine needs a key (the provider's API key). This key is too valuable—anyone holding it can drive your car and burn your fuel—so it's locked in a safe (AES-encrypted storage). The safe normally exposes only the key ring's last few digits (displaySecretKey) so you can tell which key it is, and only at the moment of ignition is the real key taken out (decrypted) and put into the engine. The key is never left bare outside—it appears only in the instant of ignition.

One engine, three consumers

The Playground looks like a standalone little tool, but underneath it reuses the exact same LLM-call core as evals and experiments—fetchLLMCompletion. Its chatCompletionHandler hands the messages you typed, the model you picked, and the tools and structuredOutputSchema you configured to this core. So Lesson 29's judge, Lesson 36's experiment, and the Playground here are essentially three consumers of one engine.

one core, three reuses: web/src/features/playground/server/chatCompletionHandler.ts:19,69-86 directly calls fetchLLMCompletion, passing tools and structuredOutputSchema. This shares the same engine with Lesson 29's judge callLLM and Lesson 36's experiment model call—credential decryption and provider adaptation written once.
web/src/features/playground/server/chatCompletionHandler.tsPlayground calls the same core
import { fetchLLMCompletion } from "@langfuse/shared/src/server";   // same as evals/experiments

const fetchLLMCompletionParams = { messages, modelParams, /* configured in the UI */
  tools,                  // from LlmTool (function-call definitions)
  structuredOutputSchema  // from LlmSchema (structured output, echoing Lesson 29)
};
if (structuredOutputSchema) {
  const result = await fetchLLMCompletion({ ...fetchLLMCompletionParams, structuredOutputSchema });
}
// the Playground is just this engine's "interactive front desk"—same engine, different use
consumerwhat it hands the enginetypical use
Playground (this lesson)messages you typed + model picked + tools/schemainteractive prompt testing, manual pre-release validation
LLM judge (Lesson 29)the compiled scoring prompt + structured-output schemaauto-scoring traces (source=EVAL)
prompt experiment (Lesson 36)the variable-filled prompt + the provider/model under testrun a run per question for comparison

The three solve different business problems, but the grind of "how to call an LLM"—picking the adapter, decrypting credentials, assembling the request, receiving streams, demanding structure—is all pressed into one fetchLLMCompletion. That's the mark of a good abstraction: the upper layers vary endlessly, the foundation stands firm.

The crown jewel: encrypted storage of the API key

To call any provider you must carry its API key. This key is the most sensitive data—it can directly spend your money and act on your account—and must never sit in plaintext in the database. Langfuse's LlmApiKeys table stores it encrypted in the secretKey field, using AES-256-GCM—an authenticated encryption: both confidential, and able to detect tampering via an authTag. It separately stores a masked displaySecretKey (only the last few digits) for the UI, so you can tell which key it is without seeing the whole thing.

authenticated encryption + masked display + decrypt-on-use: encrypt() (encryption.ts:18-35) uses AES-256-GCM, generating a random 12-byte IV each time, outputting iv:encrypted:authTag. secretKey stores ciphertext, displaySecretKey a masked string (schema.prisma:299 LlmApiKeys). ENCRYPTION_KEY is 256-bit, kept separately from the DB.
packages/shared/src/encryption/encryption.ts:18-35AES-256-GCM encrypt
export function encrypt(plainText: string): string {
  const iv = crypto.randomBytes(12);                       // random IV each time → same plaintext, different ciphertext
  const cipher = crypto.createCipheriv("aes-256-gcm",
    Buffer.from(ENCRYPTION_KEY, "hex"), iv);              // 256-bit key, kept separately from the DB
  let encrypted = cipher.update(plainText, "utf8", "hex") + cipher.final("hex");
  const authTag = cipher.getAuthTag();                     // GCM auth tag → verified on decrypt, errors on tampering
  return iv.toString("hex") + ":" + encrypted + ":" + authTag.toString("hex");
}
// stored in LlmApiKeys.secretKey; decrypted only just before calling the provider (inside fetchLLMCompletion)
wherewhat form the key takeswho can see it
database (secretKey)ciphertext iv:ciphertext:authTaga breach yields gibberish (can't decrypt without ENCRYPTION_KEY)
UI (displaySecretKey)masked string sk-…xyzyou can recognize which key, but not see the whole
just before calling the providerplaintext (momentary)exists briefly in memory only, discarded after

One key, three forms, each in its place: at rest only ciphertext, to a human only a mask, plaintext appearing only in the instant of ignition. Squeezing "the exposure surface of plaintext" to nearly zero is the core discipline of credential management.

Reusable building blocks: structured schemas and tools

Besides credentials, modern LLM calls often carry two things: a structured-output schema (requiring the model to return a fixed JSON shape, which Lesson 29's judge relies on) and tool/function definitions (letting the model "call" external capabilities). Langfuse models these as reusable entities too—LlmSchema and LlmTool—so you define them once in the Playground and reuse them in evals and experiments.

LlmSchema · structured output

Defines the JSON shape the model must obey (e.g. {score, reasoning}). This is the underlying dependency that lets Lesson 29's judge reliably return "score + reasoning"—the same schema mechanism shared by the judge and the Playground.

LlmTool · function/tool

Defines tool signatures the model can call (name, parameter schema). It lets the model demonstrate "function-calling" agent behavior right in the Playground, with the definition saved for reuse elsewhere.

LlmApiKeys · connection

Encrypted provider credentials + adapter (abstracting the openai/anthropic/etc. interface) + optional baseURL (for self-hosted/proxy endpoints). The common base for all three LLM-call sites.

These three blocks—connection, schema, tool—plus the unified fetchLLMCompletion engine, form the common foundation for every server-side LLM call in Langfuse. Here Part 7 (and indeed the first seven parts' "developer workflow" through-line) closes into a loop: interactively test a prompt in the Playground → version it (Lesson 37) once satisfied → pull it into a dataset to run experiments and compare (Lesson 36) → release safely via a label (Lesson 37) → after shipping, get observed (Parts 1–4), evaluated (Part 5), and alerted when needed (Lesson 33). A complete, data-backed, safely-iterable LLM engineering lifecycle, sealed tight.

🎯 Design trade-off
Why use "authenticated encryption" like AES-256-GCM rather than plain symmetric encryption? Because for data like an API key, confidentiality alone isn't enough—you also need anti-tampering. Plain encryption (e.g. AES-CBC) guarantees "others can't read the ciphertext", but can't stop someone quietly altering a few bytes—after which you might decrypt to another seemingly-valid garbage string, failing silently. GCM mode computes an authTag (authentication tag) alongside encryption, verified on decrypt: flip even one bit of the ciphertext and verification fails, rejecting it outright. So you get not just "confidentiality" but "integrity guarantee"—the plaintext you get is either exactly what was stored, or an outright error, never a silently-tampered version. For credentials, where "a small error brews a big disaster", authenticated encryption is the floor.

Why a random IV per encryption, and why also store a masked displaySecretKey? The random IV (initialization vector) solves "the same plaintext encrypts to different ciphertext each time"—otherwise two identical keys would have identical ciphertext, and an attacker could instantly see "these two use the same key", leaking information. A fresh random IV makes ciphertext patternless. The displaySecretKey solves a very practical UX problem: in the UI you must recognize "which key this is", yet you shouldn't decrypt and expose the whole key just to display it.
